'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Oiso-jinja}} [[EN:Oiso Shrine]] [[JA:奥石神社]] {{wikidata link|Q24864985}} == Japanese content == {{Shinto shrine |Name=Oiso Shrine |Image=[[File:Oiso-jinja, keidai.jpg|270px]]
Shrine precincts |Location={{jalink|Shiga Prefecture|Q131358}}{{jalink|Ōmihachiman City|Q385461}}{{jalink|Azuchi Town|Q165676}} Higashi-Oiso 1615 |Coordinates= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Deity={{jalink|Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|Q2564508|Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto}} |Shintai= |Shrine rank={{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (minor)
Former {{jalink|Prefectural Shrine}} |Founded=Traditionally: Year 5 of {{jalink|Emperor Kōrei|Q313041}} |Main hall=Three-bay {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} |Alternate name=Kama-no-miya Shrine |Pilgrimage= |Annual festival=An auspicious day in April |Rituals= |Map= {{Location map|Japan Shiga|label=Oiso Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=250}} }} [[File:Oiso-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii gate}}]] '''Oiso Shrine''' (おいそじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} located in Higashi-Oiso, {{jalink|Azuchi Town|Q165676}}, {{jalink|Ōmihachiman City|Q385461}}, {{jalink|Shiga Prefecture|Q131358}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}, and under the {{jalink|modern system of shrine rankings|Q712534|former ranking}} it was a {{jalink|Prefectural Shrine}}. It is enshrined within Oisonomori (老蘇森), a nationally designated {{jalink|Historic Site|Q1081138}}, along the {{jalink|Nakasendō|Q1154479}} (also called the Nakasendō), north of the Higashi-Oiso settlement. === Enshrined deity === The currently enshrined deity is the following single kamiExplanation board within the precincts.. * {{jalink|Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|Q2564508|Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto}} The various deities enshrined in the main hall are hearth-fire prevention deities. Because the character for “kama” (sickle) was applied to “kamado” (hearth), the shrine came to be called “Kama Daimyōjin” or “Kama-no-miya.” Even today, parishioners maintain the custom of offering sickles to the shrine{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. === History === === Foundation === According to the *Oiso Shrine Honki* (colophon dated September of the first year of {{jalink|Shitoku (Japan)|Q1146202|Shitoku}} ({{jalink|1384|Q6347}})), the shrine began in the 5th year of the 7th emperor, {{jalink|Emperor Kōrei|Q313041}}, when Ishibe no Ōmuraji built a sacred altar in Oisonomori and enshrined the child deity of Ōtoshi-no-Kami{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. It further states that during the reign of the 10th emperor, {{jalink|Emperor Sujin|Q333530}}, {{jalink|Kibitsuhiko}} established a palace and enshrined Okutsuhiko-no-Mikoto and Okutsuhime-no-Mikoto, and that during the reign of the 15th emperor, {{jalink|Emperor Ōjin|Q317997}}, Kasa no Ason (a descendant of Kibitsuhiko) additionally enshrined {{jalink|Yamato Takeru|Q461258|Yamato Takeru}} and {{jalink|Oto Tachibana-hime|Q11487396|Tachibana-hime}}{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. One theory holds that the shrine originated in primitive mountain worship with Mount Kinugasa as the {{jalink|shintai-zan|Q1595289}} (sacred mountain), and that the present site is a village shrine from which the {{jalink|iwakura|Q11587880}} at the mountain summit is worshiped from afar{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. === Overview === In the *{{jalink|Shinshō Kakuchōfushō|Q11502312}}* record dated the first year of {{jalink|Daido (Japan)|Q1373191|Daidō}} ({{jalink|806|Q30021}}), one household from Ōmi Province was assigned as {{jalink|shinko (household)|Q11589711}} to the then “Oiso Deity”{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The *Oiso Shrine Honki* also records that in the first year of {{jalink|Ninju|Q1373077}} ({{jalink|851|Q29966}}), the shrine was granted the divine rank of Senior Sixth Rank Upper{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. In the *{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}* {{jalink|Register of Deities|Q11064932}} compiled in the 5th year of {{jalink|Enchō|Q1327274|Enchō}} ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}), it is listed as “Oiso Shrine” in {{jalink|Gamo District|Q931658}}, {{jalink|Ōmi Province|Q843882}}, and is ranked as a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. In the 9th year of {{jalink|Tenshō|Q1145058}} ({{jalink|1581|Q6717}}), the main hall was rebuilt. In the {{jalink|early modern period|Q5308718}}, it was known by names such as “Kama Daimyōjin” and “Kama-no-miya Shrine”{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. After the {{jalink|Meiji Restoration|Q8707}}, under the {{jalink|modern shrine ranking system|Q712534}}, it was designated a village shrine in October {{jalink|1876|Q7766}} ({{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} 9){{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. It was promoted to a township shrine in February {{jalink|1881|Q7808}} (Meiji 14), designated a shrine receiving state offerings in April {{jalink|1908|Q2056}} (Meiji 41), and in February {{jalink|1924|Q2173}} ({{jalink|Taishō|Q329174}} 13) promoted to a {{jalink|Prefectural Shrine}} and renamed its current name, “Oiso Shrine” [http://www.shiga-jinjacho.jp/ycBBS/Board.cgi/02_jinja_db/db/ycDB_02jinja-pc-detail.html?mode:view=1&view:oid=653 Oiso Shrine] (Shiga Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines).. During the {{jalink|period of rapid economic growth|Q1135329}}, parts of the precincts were taken for the {{jalink|National Route 8|Q640239}} bypass and the {{jalink|Tōkaidō Shinkansen|Q660895}}, resulting in the precincts being divided. === Precincts === [[File:Oiso-jinja, honden.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Main hall (right, a National {{jalink|Important Cultural Property|Q1188622}})
Suwa Shrine (left, a cultural property designated by Ōmihachiman City)}}]] [[File:Oiso-jinja, sandou.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Approach and shrine grove (Oisonomori)}}]] * Main hall ({{jalink|Important Cultural Property|Q1188622}}) – Rebuilt in the 9th year of {{jalink|Tenshō|Q1145058}} ({{jalink|1581|Q6717}}). It is a three-bay {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} structure with a one-bay front porch and a cypress-bark roof. It features splendid decorations such as openwork arabesque carvings on the {{jalink|kamoi brackets|Q123117911}}, carvings on the {{jalink|tesaki|Q124660898}}, and latticework panels around the lower walls of the core. According to the {{jalink|ridge tag|Q8008834}}, it was constructed by Shibata Iehisa, and a connection has been suggested with policies related to the formation of the castle town of {{jalink|Azuchi Castle|Q786888}} under {{jalink|Oda Nobunaga|Q171411}}Explanation board for the Oiso Shrine main hall within the precincts.. * Middle gate * Worship hall * Kagura hall * Shrine office === Oisonomori === A nationally designated {{jalink|Historic Site|Q1081138}}. The shrine grove surrounding the buildings is called “Oisonomori.” The present forest consists mainly of {{jalink|cedar|Q147388}}, {{jalink|cypress|Q917187}}, and {{jalink|pine|Q12024}}. According to tradition in the *Oiso Shrine Honki*, the land was once split open with water welling up and was uninhabitable, but when Ishibe no Ōmuraji planted saplings and prayed to the gods, it became a great forest; Ishibe no Ōmuraji lived to over a hundred years, and thus it came to be called the “Oiso (Old and Revived) Forest.” The name appears in a poem from the first year of {{jalink|Manju|Q1194155}} ({{jalink|1024|Q19265}}) in the {{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}}, and it is also introduced as a {{jalink|utamakura|Q1588579}} of Ōmi Province in works such as the *{{jalink|Ryōjin Hishō|Q3010584}}*, *Nōin Utamakura*, and *Waka Shogakushō*. Because it lay along the medieval {{jalink|Tōkaidō|Q1196306}} and the early modern Nakasendō, it is also mentioned in travelogues from the {{jalink|Kamakura period|Q236205}} onward, such as the *{{jalink|Tōkan Kikō|Q11528660}}*. For these reasons, as a place of long-standing and valuable history, the forest area has been designated a National Historic Site{{Sfn|Oisonomori (Heibonsha)|1991}}Explanation board for Oisonomori within the precincts.. File:Oiso-jinja, haiden.jpg|Worship hall File:Oiso-jinja, kaguraden.jpg|Kagura hall File:Oiso-jinja, enkei.jpg|Shrine grove (Oisonomori) seen from the former {{jalink|Nakasendō|Q1154479}} === Subsidiary shrines === * Suwa Shrine (Tangible Cultural Property designated by Ōmihachiman City) *: Enshrined deities: {{jalink|Takeminakata|Q9150391|Takeminakata-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Yasakatome|Q9150392|Yasakatome-no-Mikoto}}. Located to the west of the main hall. Although the exact construction date is unknown, it is thought to have been built around the Azuchi–Momoyama period. It is a one-bay {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} structure with a cypress-bark roof. Repairs were carried out in the 7th year of {{jalink|Ansei|Q1327259}} ({{jalink|1860|Q7691}}) and in {{jalink|1880|Q7806}} (Meiji 13), and it is regarded as a building that retains much of its original materials in good conditionExplanation board for the Suwa Shrine main hall within the Oiso Shrine precincts.. * Yoshizumi Inari Shrine File:Oiso-jinja, Yoshizumi-Inarisha.jpg|Yoshizumi Inari Shrine === Cultural properties === === Important Cultural Properties === * Main hall (structure) – Built in the Azuchi–Momoyama period, Tenshō 9 ({{jalink|1581|Q6717}}). Designated on April 17, {{jalink|1902|Q2043}} (Meiji 35){{National Cultural Properties Database|102|1474|Oiso Shrine Main Hall}}. === National Historic Sites === * Oisonomori – Designated on July 13, {{jalink|1949|Q5188}} ({{jalink|Shōwa|Q327567}} 24){{National Cultural Properties Database|401|1590|Oisonomori}}. === Cultural properties designated by Ōmihachiman City === * Tangible Cultural Properties ** Suwa Shrine main hall (structure) – Built around the Azuchi–Momoyama period. === Appearances in literature === * From the *{{jalink|Goshūi Wakashū}}*, a poem composed by Ōe no Kinsuke, Governor of Sagami, upon departing for his post in the first year of {{jalink|Manju|Q1194155}} ({{jalink|1024|Q19265}}) {{Cquote|On the eastern road,
I shall keep it as a memory—
the cuckoo,
one cry in the deep night
of '''Oisonomori'''|20px||*{{jalink|Goshūi Wakashū}}* {{Sfn|Oisonomori (Heibonsha)|1991}}}} === On-site information === '''Address''' * Higashi-Oiso 1615, {{jalink|Azuchi Town|Q165676}}, {{jalink|Ōmihachiman City|Q385461}}, {{jalink|Shiga Prefecture|Q131358}} '''Access''' * About 15 minutes by {{jalink|Akakon Bus|Q17190918}} from {{jalink|Azuchi Station|Q649689}} on the {{jalink|JR West|Q502125|JR}} {{jalink|Tōkaidō Main Line|Q1190152}} ({{jalink|Biwako Line|Q804135}}); get off at the Oiso Shrine stop. === Notes === {{Help notes}} {{Reflist|2}} === References === * On-site explanation boards * {{Cite book|Japanese|editor=Edited by the Meiji Shrine Records Compilation Office|author=|year=1912|chapter=Kama-no-miya Shrine|title={{jalink|Fuken Gōsha Meiji Jinja Shiryō|Q11486103}}|publisher=Meiji Shrine Records Compilation Office|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Meiji Shrine Records|1912}}}} ** [{{NDLDC|1088278/101}} *Meiji Shrine Records: Prefectural and Township Shrines, Vol. 1*] (National Diet Library Digital Collection), reference frame 101. * {{Cite book|Japanese|editor=|author=|year=1991|chapter=|title={{jalink|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|Q17213509}} 25 Place Names of Shiga Prefecture|publisher={{jalink|Heibonsha|Q11482108}}|isbn=4582490255|ref=}} ** {{Wikicite|reference="Oiso Shrine"|ref={{Harvid|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}}}, {{Wikicite|reference="Oisonomori"|ref={{Harvid|Oisonomori (Heibonsha)|1991}}}}. * {{PDFlink|[http://www.shiga-ec.ed.jp/www/contents/1438304524592/html/common/other/55d173d3029.pdf “Oisonomori”]}} Shiga Prefectural Board of Education, Cultural Properties Protection Division, 2004 (Shiga Prefectural Comprehensive Education Center “Shiga Prefecture Cultural Properties Learning Sheet”). === External links === {{Commonscat|Oiso-jinja}} * [http://www.shiga-jinjacho.jp/ycBBS/Board.cgi/02_jinja_db/db/ycDB_02jinja-pc-detail.html?mode:view=1&view:oid=653 Oiso Shrine] – Shiga Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines {{Redirect category membership |redirect1=Oisonomori |1-1=Ōmihachiman City |1-2=National Historic Sites in Shiga Prefecture }} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Oiso Shrine}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages]] [[Category:Pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ōmi Province]] [[Category:滋賀県の神社]] [[Category:近江八幡市の建築物]] [[Category:近江八幡市の歴史]] [[Category:近江国の式内社]] [[Category:滋賀県の旧県社]] [[Category:近江八幡市の重要文化財]] [[Category:pages]]

'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Oiso-jinja}} [[EN:Oiso Shrine]] [[JA:奥石神社]] {{wikidata link|Q24864985}} == Japanese content == {{Shinto shrine |Name=Oiso Shrine |Image=[[File:Oiso-jinja, keidai.jpg|270px]]
Shrine precincts |Location={{jalink|Shiga Prefecture|Q131358}}{{jalink|Ōmihachiman City|Q385461}}{{jalink|Azuchi Town|Q165676}} Higashi-Oiso 1615 |Coordinates= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Deity={{jalink|Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|Q2564508|Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto}} |Shintai= |Shrine rank={{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (minor)
Former {{jalink|Prefectural Shrine}} |Founded=Traditionally: Year 5 of {{jalink|Emperor Kōrei|Q313041}} |Main hall=Three-bay {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} |Alternate name=Kama-no-miya Shrine |Pilgrimage= |Annual festival=An auspicious day in April |Rituals= |Map= {{Location map|Japan Shiga|label=Oiso Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=250}} }} [[File:Oiso-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii gate}}]] '''Oiso Shrine''' (おいそじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} located in Higashi-Oiso, {{jalink|Azuchi Town|Q165676}}, {{jalink|Ōmihachiman City|Q385461}}, {{jalink|Shiga Prefecture|Q131358}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}, and under the {{jalink|modern system of shrine rankings|Q712534|former ranking}} it was a {{jalink|Prefectural Shrine}}. It is enshrined within Oisonomori (老蘇森), a nationally designated {{jalink|Historic Site|Q1081138}}, along the {{jalink|Nakasendō|Q1154479}} (also called the Nakasendō), north of the Higashi-Oiso settlement. === Enshrined deity === The currently enshrined deity is the following single kamiExplanation board within the precincts.. * {{jalink|Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|Q2564508|Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto}} The various deities enshrined in the main hall are hearth-fire prevention deities. Because the character for “kama” (sickle) was applied to “kamado” (hearth), the shrine came to be called “Kama Daimyōjin” or “Kama-no-miya.” Even today, parishioners maintain the custom of offering sickles to the shrine{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. === History === === Foundation === According to the *Oiso Shrine Honki* (colophon dated September of the first year of {{jalink|Shitoku (Japan)|Q1146202|Shitoku}} ({{jalink|1384|Q6347}})), the shrine began in the 5th year of the 7th emperor, {{jalink|Emperor Kōrei|Q313041}}, when Ishibe no Ōmuraji built a sacred altar in Oisonomori and enshrined the child deity of Ōtoshi-no-Kami{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. It further states that during the reign of the 10th emperor, {{jalink|Emperor Sujin|Q333530}}, {{jalink|Kibitsuhiko}} established a palace and enshrined Okutsuhiko-no-Mikoto and Okutsuhime-no-Mikoto, and that during the reign of the 15th emperor, {{jalink|Emperor Ōjin|Q317997}}, Kasa no Ason (a descendant of Kibitsuhiko) additionally enshrined {{jalink|Yamato Takeru|Q461258|Yamato Takeru}} and {{jalink|Oto Tachibana-hime|Q11487396|Tachibana-hime}}{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. One theory holds that the shrine originated in primitive mountain worship with Mount Kinugasa as the {{jalink|shintai-zan|Q1595289}} (sacred mountain), and that the present site is a village shrine from which the {{jalink|iwakura|Q11587880}} at the mountain summit is worshiped from afar{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. === Overview === In the *{{jalink|Shinshō Kakuchōfushō|Q11502312}}* record dated the first year of {{jalink|Daido (Japan)|Q1373191|Daidō}} ({{jalink|806|Q30021}}), one household from Ōmi Province was assigned as {{jalink|shinko (household)|Q11589711}} to the then “Oiso Deity”{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The *Oiso Shrine Honki* also records that in the first year of {{jalink|Ninju|Q1373077}} ({{jalink|851|Q29966}}), the shrine was granted the divine rank of Senior Sixth Rank Upper{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. In the *{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}* {{jalink|Register of Deities|Q11064932}} compiled in the 5th year of {{jalink|Enchō|Q1327274|Enchō}} ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}), it is listed as “Oiso Shrine” in {{jalink|Gamo District|Q931658}}, {{jalink|Ōmi Province|Q843882}}, and is ranked as a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. In the 9th year of {{jalink|Tenshō|Q1145058}} ({{jalink|1581|Q6717}}), the main hall was rebuilt. In the {{jalink|early modern period|Q5308718}}, it was known by names such as “Kama Daimyōjin” and “Kama-no-miya Shrine”{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. After the {{jalink|Meiji Restoration|Q8707}}, under the {{jalink|modern shrine ranking system|Q712534}}, it was designated a village shrine in October {{jalink|1876|Q7766}} ({{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} 9){{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. It was promoted to a township shrine in February {{jalink|1881|Q7808}} (Meiji 14), designated a shrine receiving state offerings in April {{jalink|1908|Q2056}} (Meiji 41), and in February {{jalink|1924|Q2173}} ({{jalink|Taishō|Q329174}} 13) promoted to a {{jalink|Prefectural Shrine}} and renamed its current name, “Oiso Shrine” [http://www.shiga-jinjacho.jp/ycBBS/Board.cgi/02_jinja_db/db/ycDB_02jinja-pc-detail.html?mode:view=1&view:oid=653 Oiso Shrine] (Shiga Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines).. During the {{jalink|period of rapid economic growth|Q1135329}}, parts of the precincts were taken for the {{jalink|National Route 8|Q640239}} bypass and the {{jalink|Tōkaidō Shinkansen|Q660895}}, resulting in the precincts being divided. === Precincts === [[File:Oiso-jinja, honden.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Main hall (right, a National {{jalink|Important Cultural Property|Q1188622}})
Suwa Shrine (left, a cultural property designated by Ōmihachiman City)}}]] [[File:Oiso-jinja, sandou.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Approach and shrine grove (Oisonomori)}}]] * Main hall ({{jalink|Important Cultural Property|Q1188622}}) – Rebuilt in the 9th year of {{jalink|Tenshō|Q1145058}} ({{jalink|1581|Q6717}}). It is a three-bay {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} structure with a one-bay front porch and a cypress-bark roof. It features splendid decorations such as openwork arabesque carvings on the {{jalink|kamoi brackets|Q123117911}}, carvings on the {{jalink|tesaki|Q124660898}}, and latticework panels around the lower walls of the core. According to the {{jalink|ridge tag|Q8008834}}, it was constructed by Shibata Iehisa, and a connection has been suggested with policies related to the formation of the castle town of {{jalink|Azuchi Castle|Q786888}} under {{jalink|Oda Nobunaga|Q171411}}Explanation board for the Oiso Shrine main hall within the precincts.. * Middle gate * Worship hall * Kagura hall * Shrine office === Oisonomori === A nationally designated {{jalink|Historic Site|Q1081138}}. The shrine grove surrounding the buildings is called “Oisonomori.” The present forest consists mainly of {{jalink|cedar|Q147388}}, {{jalink|cypress|Q917187}}, and {{jalink|pine|Q12024}}. According to tradition in the *Oiso Shrine Honki*, the land was once split open with water welling up and was uninhabitable, but when Ishibe no Ōmuraji planted saplings and prayed to the gods, it became a great forest; Ishibe no Ōmuraji lived to over a hundred years, and thus it came to be called the “Oiso (Old and Revived) Forest.” The name appears in a poem from the first year of {{jalink|Manju|Q1194155}} ({{jalink|1024|Q19265}}) in the {{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}}, and it is also introduced as a {{jalink|utamakura|Q1588579}} of Ōmi Province in works such as the *{{jalink|Ryōjin Hishō|Q3010584}}*, *Nōin Utamakura*, and *Waka Shogakushō*. Because it lay along the medieval {{jalink|Tōkaidō|Q1196306}} and the early modern Nakasendō, it is also mentioned in travelogues from the {{jalink|Kamakura period|Q236205}} onward, such as the *{{jalink|Tōkan Kikō|Q11528660}}*. For these reasons, as a place of long-standing and valuable history, the forest area has been designated a National Historic Site{{Sfn|Oisonomori (Heibonsha)|1991}}Explanation board for Oisonomori within the precincts.. File:Oiso-jinja, haiden.jpg|Worship hall File:Oiso-jinja, kaguraden.jpg|Kagura hall File:Oiso-jinja, enkei.jpg|Shrine grove (Oisonomori) seen from the former {{jalink|Nakasendō|Q1154479}} === Subsidiary shrines === * Suwa Shrine (Tangible Cultural Property designated by Ōmihachiman City) *: Enshrined deities: {{jalink|Takeminakata|Q9150391|Takeminakata-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Yasakatome|Q9150392|Yasakatome-no-Mikoto}}. Located to the west of the main hall. Although the exact construction date is unknown, it is thought to have been built around the Azuchi–Momoyama period. It is a one-bay {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} structure with a cypress-bark roof. Repairs were carried out in the 7th year of {{jalink|Ansei|Q1327259}} ({{jalink|1860|Q7691}}) and in {{jalink|1880|Q7806}} (Meiji 13), and it is regarded as a building that retains much of its original materials in good conditionExplanation board for the Suwa Shrine main hall within the Oiso Shrine precincts.. * Yoshizumi Inari Shrine File:Oiso-jinja, Yoshizumi-Inarisha.jpg|Yoshizumi Inari Shrine === Cultural properties === === Important Cultural Properties === * Main hall (structure) – Built in the Azuchi–Momoyama period, Tenshō 9 ({{jalink|1581|Q6717}}). Designated on April 17, {{jalink|1902|Q2043}} (Meiji 35){{National Cultural Properties Database|102|1474|Oiso Shrine Main Hall}}. === National Historic Sites === * Oisonomori – Designated on July 13, {{jalink|1949|Q5188}} ({{jalink|Shōwa|Q327567}} 24){{National Cultural Properties Database|401|1590|Oisonomori}}. === Cultural properties designated by Ōmihachiman City === * Tangible Cultural Properties ** Suwa Shrine main hall (structure) – Built around the Azuchi–Momoyama period. === Appearances in literature === * From the *{{jalink|Goshūi Wakashū}}*, a poem composed by Ōe no Kinsuke, Governor of Sagami, upon departing for his post in the first year of {{jalink|Manju|Q1194155}} ({{jalink|1024|Q19265}}) {{Cquote|On the eastern road,
I shall keep it as a memory—
the cuckoo,
one cry in the deep night
of '''Oisonomori'''|20px||*{{jalink|Goshūi Wakashū}}* {{Sfn|Oisonomori (Heibonsha)|1991}}}} === On-site information === '''Address''' * Higashi-Oiso 1615, {{jalink|Azuchi Town|Q165676}}, {{jalink|Ōmihachiman City|Q385461}}, {{jalink|Shiga Prefecture|Q131358}} '''Access''' * About 15 minutes by {{jalink|Akakon Bus|Q17190918}} from {{jalink|Azuchi Station|Q649689}} on the {{jalink|JR West|Q502125|JR}} {{jalink|Tōkaidō Main Line|Q1190152}} ({{jalink|Biwako Line|Q804135}}); get off at the Oiso Shrine stop. === Notes === {{Help notes}} {{Reflist|2}} === References === * On-site 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Oiso-jinja}} [[EN:Oiso Shrine]] [[JA:奥石神社]] {{wikidata link|Q24864985}} == Japanese content == {{Shinto shrine |Name=Oiso Shrine |Image=[[File:Oiso-jinja, keidai.jpg|270px]]
Shrine precincts |Location={{jalink|Shiga Prefecture|Q131358}}{{jalink|Ōmihachiman City|Q385461}}{{jalink|Azuchi Town|Q165676}} Higashi-Oiso 1615 |Coordinates= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Deity={{jalink|Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|Q2564508|Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto}} |Shintai= |Shrine rank={{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (minor)
Former {{jalink|Prefectural Shrine}} |Founded=Traditionally: Year 5 of {{jalink|Emperor Kōrei|Q313041}} |Main hall=Three-bay {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} |Alternate name=Kama-no-miya Shrine |Pilgrimage= |Annual festival=An auspicious day in April |Rituals= |Map= {{Location map|Japan Shiga|label=Oiso Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=250}} }} [[File:Oiso-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii gate}}]] '''Oiso Shrine''' (おいそじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} located in Higashi-Oiso, {{jalink|Azuchi Town|Q165676}}, {{jalink|Ōmihachiman City|Q385461}}, {{jalink|Shiga Prefecture|Q131358}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}, and under the {{jalink|modern system of shrine rankings|Q712534|former ranking}} it was a {{jalink|Prefectural Shrine}}. It is enshrined within Oisonomori (老蘇森), a nationally designated {{jalink|Historic Site|Q1081138}}, along the {{jalink|Nakasendō|Q1154479}} (also called the Nakasendō), north of the Higashi-Oiso settlement. === Enshrined deity === The currently enshrined deity is the following single kamiExplanation board within the precincts.. * {{jalink|Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|Q2564508|Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto}} The various deities enshrined in the main hall are hearth-fire prevention deities. Because the character for “kama” (sickle) was applied to “kamado” (hearth), the shrine came to be called “Kama Daimyōjin” or “Kama-no-miya.” Even today, parishioners maintain the custom of offering sickles to the shrine{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. === History === === Foundation === According to the *Oiso Shrine Honki* (colophon dated September of the first year of {{jalink|Shitoku (Japan)|Q1146202|Shitoku}} ({{jalink|1384|Q6347}})), the shrine began in the 5th year of the 7th emperor, {{jalink|Emperor Kōrei|Q313041}}, when Ishibe no Ōmuraji built a sacred altar in Oisonomori and enshrined the child deity of Ōtoshi-no-Kami{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. It further states that during the reign of the 10th emperor, {{jalink|Emperor Sujin|Q333530}}, {{jalink|Kibitsuhiko}} established a palace and enshrined Okutsuhiko-no-Mikoto and Okutsuhime-no-Mikoto, and that during the reign of the 15th emperor, {{jalink|Emperor Ōjin|Q317997}}, Kasa no Ason (a descendant of Kibitsuhiko) additionally enshrined {{jalink|Yamato Takeru|Q461258|Yamato Takeru}} and {{jalink|Oto Tachibana-hime|Q11487396|Tachibana-hime}}{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. One theory holds that the shrine originated in primitive mountain worship with Mount Kinugasa as the {{jalink|shintai-zan|Q1595289}} (sacred mountain), and that the present site is a village shrine from which the {{jalink|iwakura|Q11587880}} at the mountain summit is worshiped from afar{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. === Overview === In the *{{jalink|Shinshō Kakuchōfushō|Q11502312}}* record dated the first year of {{jalink|Daido (Japan)|Q1373191|Daidō}} ({{jalink|806|Q30021}}), one household from Ōmi Province was assigned as {{jalink|shinko (household)|Q11589711}} to the then “Oiso Deity”{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The *Oiso Shrine Honki* also records that in the first year of {{jalink|Ninju|Q1373077}} ({{jalink|851|Q29966}}), the shrine was granted the divine rank of Senior Sixth Rank Upper{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. In the *{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}* {{jalink|Register of Deities|Q11064932}} compiled in the 5th year of {{jalink|Enchō|Q1327274|Enchō}} ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}), it is listed as “Oiso Shrine” in {{jalink|Gamo District|Q931658}}, {{jalink|Ōmi Province|Q843882}}, and is ranked as a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. In the 9th year of {{jalink|Tenshō|Q1145058}} ({{jalink|1581|Q6717}}), the main hall was rebuilt. In the {{jalink|early modern period|Q5308718}}, it was known by names such as “Kama Daimyōjin” and “Kama-no-miya Shrine”{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. After the {{jalink|Meiji Restoration|Q8707}}, under the {{jalink|modern shrine ranking system|Q712534}}, it was designated a village shrine in October {{jalink|1876|Q7766}} ({{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} 9){{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. It was promoted to a township shrine in February {{jalink|1881|Q7808}} (Meiji 14), designated a shrine receiving state offerings in April {{jalink|1908|Q2056}} (Meiji 41), and in February {{jalink|1924|Q2173}} ({{jalink|Taishō|Q329174}} 13) promoted to a {{jalink|Prefectural Shrine}} and renamed its current name, “Oiso Shrine” [http://www.shiga-jinjacho.jp/ycBBS/Board.cgi/02_jinja_db/db/ycDB_02jinja-pc-detail.html?mode:view=1&view:oid=653 Oiso Shrine] (Shiga Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines).. During the {{jalink|period of rapid economic growth|Q1135329}}, parts of the precincts were taken for the {{jalink|National Route 8|Q640239}} bypass and the {{jalink|Tōkaidō Shinkansen|Q660895}}, resulting in the precincts being divided. === Precincts === [[File:Oiso-jinja, honden.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Main hall (right, a National {{jalink|Important Cultural Property|Q1188622}})
Suwa Shrine (left, a cultural property designated by Ōmihachiman City)}}]] [[File:Oiso-jinja, sandou.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Approach and shrine grove (Oisonomori)}}]] * Main hall ({{jalink|Important Cultural Property|Q1188622}}) – Rebuilt in the 9th year of {{jalink|Tenshō|Q1145058}} ({{jalink|1581|Q6717}}). It is a three-bay {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} structure with a one-bay front porch and a cypress-bark roof. It features splendid decorations such as openwork arabesque carvings on the {{jalink|kamoi brackets|Q123117911}}, carvings on the {{jalink|tesaki|Q124660898}}, and latticework panels around the lower walls of the core. According to the {{jalink|ridge tag|Q8008834}}, it was constructed by Shibata Iehisa, and a connection has been suggested with policies related to the formation of the castle town of {{jalink|Azuchi Castle|Q786888}} under {{jalink|Oda Nobunaga|Q171411}}Explanation board for the Oiso Shrine main hall within the precincts.. * Middle gate * Worship hall * Kagura hall * Shrine office === Oisonomori === A nationally designated {{jalink|Historic Site|Q1081138}}. The shrine grove surrounding the buildings is called “Oisonomori.” The present forest consists mainly of {{jalink|cedar|Q147388}}, {{jalink|cypress|Q917187}}, and {{jalink|pine|Q12024}}. According to tradition in the *Oiso Shrine Honki*, the land was once split open with water welling up and was uninhabitable, but when Ishibe no Ōmuraji planted saplings and prayed to the gods, it became a great forest; Ishibe no Ōmuraji lived to over a hundred years, and thus it came to be called the “Oiso (Old and Revived) Forest.” The name appears in a poem from the first year of {{jalink|Manju|Q1194155}} ({{jalink|1024|Q19265}}) in the {{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}}, and it is also introduced as a {{jalink|utamakura|Q1588579}} of Ōmi Province in works such as the *{{jalink|Ryōjin Hishō|Q3010584}}*, *Nōin Utamakura*, and *Waka Shogakushō*. Because it lay along the medieval {{jalink|Tōkaidō|Q1196306}} and the early modern Nakasendō, it is also mentioned in travelogues from the {{jalink|Kamakura period|Q236205}} onward, such as the *{{jalink|Tōkan Kikō|Q11528660}}*. For these reasons, as a place of long-standing and valuable history, the forest area has been designated a National Historic Site{{Sfn|Oisonomori (Heibonsha)|1991}}Explanation board for Oisonomori within the precincts.. File:Oiso-jinja, haiden.jpg|Worship hall File:Oiso-jinja, kaguraden.jpg|Kagura hall File:Oiso-jinja, enkei.jpg|Shrine grove (Oisonomori) seen from the former {{jalink|Nakasendō|Q1154479}} === Subsidiary shrines === * Suwa Shrine (Tangible Cultural Property designated by Ōmihachiman City) *: Enshrined deities: {{jalink|Takeminakata|Q9150391|Takeminakata-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Yasakatome|Q9150392|Yasakatome-no-Mikoto}}. Located to the west of the main hall. Although the exact construction date is unknown, it is thought to have been built around the Azuchi–Momoyama period. It is a one-bay {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} structure with a cypress-bark roof. Repairs were carried out in the 7th year of {{jalink|Ansei|Q1327259}} ({{jalink|1860|Q7691}}) and in {{jalink|1880|Q7806}} (Meiji 13), and it is regarded as a building that retains much of its original materials in good conditionExplanation board for the Suwa Shrine main hall within the Oiso Shrine precincts.. * Yoshizumi Inari Shrine File:Oiso-jinja, Yoshizumi-Inarisha.jpg|Yoshizumi Inari Shrine === Cultural properties === === Important Cultural Properties === * Main hall (structure) – Built in the Azuchi–Momoyama period, Tenshō 9 ({{jalink|1581|Q6717}}). Designated on April 17, {{jalink|1902|Q2043}} (Meiji 35){{National Cultural Properties Database|102|1474|Oiso Shrine Main Hall}}. === National Historic Sites === * Oisonomori – Designated on July 13, {{jalink|1949|Q5188}} ({{jalink|Shōwa|Q327567}} 24){{National Cultural Properties Database|401|1590|Oisonomori}}. === Cultural properties designated by Ōmihachiman City === * Tangible Cultural Properties ** Suwa Shrine main hall (structure) – Built around the Azuchi–Momoyama period. === Appearances in literature === * From the *{{jalink|Goshūi Wakashū}}*, a poem composed by Ōe no Kinsuke, Governor of Sagami, upon departing for his post in the first year of {{jalink|Manju|Q1194155}} ({{jalink|1024|Q19265}}) {{Cquote|On the eastern road,
I shall keep it as a memory—
the cuckoo,
one cry in the deep night
of '''Oisonomori'''|20px||*{{jalink|Goshūi Wakashū}}* {{Sfn|Oisonomori (Heibonsha)|1991}}}} === On-site information === '''Address''' * Higashi-Oiso 1615, {{jalink|Azuchi Town|Q165676}}, {{jalink|Ōmihachiman City|Q385461}}, {{jalink|Shiga Prefecture|Q131358}} '''Access''' * About 15 minutes by {{jalink|Akakon Bus|Q17190918}} from {{jalink|Azuchi Station|Q649689}} on the {{jalink|JR West|Q502125|JR}} {{jalink|Tōkaidō Main Line|Q1190152}} ({{jalink|Biwako Line|Q804135}}); get off at the Oiso Shrine stop. === Notes === {{Help notes}} {{Reflist|2}} === References === * On-site In the *{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}* {{jalink|Register of Deities|Q11064932}} compiled in the 5th year of {{jalink|Enchō|Q1327274|Enchō}} ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}), it is listed as “Oiso Shrine” in {{jalink|Gamo District|Q931658}}, {{jalink|Ōmi Province|Q843882}}, and is ranked as a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. In the 9th year of {{jalink|Tenshō|Q1145058}} ({{jalink|1581|Q6717}}), the main hall was rebuilt. In the {{jalink|early modern period|Q5308718}}, it was known by names such as “Kama Daimyōjin” and “Kama-no-miya Shrine”{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. After the {{jalink|Meiji Restoration|Q8707}}, under the {{jalink|modern shrine ranking system|Q712534}}, it was designated a village shrine in October {{jalink|1876|Q7766}} ({{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} 9){{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. It was promoted to a township shrine in February {{jalink|1881|Q7808}} (Meiji 14), designated a shrine receiving state offerings in April {{jalink|1908|Q2056}} (Meiji 41), and in February {{jalink|1924|Q2173}} ({{jalink|Taishō|Q329174}} 13) promoted to a {{jalink|Prefectural Shrine}} and renamed its current name, “Oiso Shrine” [http://www.shiga-jinjacho.jp/ycBBS/Board.cgi/02_jinja_db/db/ycDB_02jinja-pc-detail.html?mode:view=1&view:oid=653 Oiso Shrine] (Shiga Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines).. During the {{jalink|period of rapid economic growth|Q1135329}}, parts of the precincts were taken for the {{jalink|National Route 8|Q640239}} bypass and the {{jalink|Tōkaidō Shinkansen|Q660895}}, resulting in the precincts being divided. === Precincts === [[File:Oiso-jinja, honden.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Main hall (right, a National {{jalink|Important Cultural Property|Q1188622}})
Suwa Shrine (left, a cultural property designated by Ōmihachiman City)}}]] [[File:Oiso-jinja, sandou.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Approach and shrine grove (Oisonomori)}}]] * Main hall ({{jalink|Important Cultural Property|Q1188622}}) – Rebuilt in the 9th year of {{jalink|Tenshō|Q1145058}} ({{jalink|1581|Q6717}}). It is a three-bay {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} structure with a one-bay front porch and a cypress-bark roof. It features splendid decorations such as openwork arabesque carvings on the {{jalink|kamoi brackets|Q123117911}}, carvings on the {{jalink|tesaki|Q124660898}}, and latticework panels around the lower walls of the core. According to the {{jalink|ridge tag|Q8008834}}, it was constructed by Shibata Iehisa, and a connection has been suggested with policies related to the formation of the castle town of {{jalink|Azuchi Castle|Q786888}} under {{jalink|Oda Nobunaga|Q171411}}Explanation board for the Oiso Shrine main hall within the precincts.. * Middle gate * Worship hall * Kagura hall * Shrine office === Oisonomori === A nationally designated {{jalink|Historic Site|Q1081138}}. The shrine grove surrounding the buildings is called “Oisonomori.” The present forest consists mainly of {{jalink|cedar|Q147388}}, {{jalink|cypress|Q917187}}, and {{jalink|pine|Q12024}}. According to tradition in the *Oiso Shrine Honki*, the land was once split open with water welling up and was uninhabitable, but when Ishibe no Ōmuraji planted saplings and prayed to the gods, it became a great forest; Ishibe no Ōmuraji lived to over a hundred years, and thus it came to be called the “Oiso (Old and Revived) Forest.” The name appears in a poem from the first year of {{jalink|Manju|Q1194155}} ({{jalink|1024|Q19265}}) in the {{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}}, and it is also introduced as a {{jalink|utamakura|Q1588579}} of Ōmi Province in works such as the *{{jalink|Ryōjin Hishō|Q3010584}}*, *Nōin Utamakura*, and *Waka Shogakushō*. Because it lay along the medieval {{jalink|Tōkaidō|Q1196306}} and the early modern Nakasendō, it is also mentioned in travelogues from the {{jalink|Kamakura period|Q236205}} onward, such as the *{{jalink|Tōkan Kikō|Q11528660}}*. For these reasons, as a place of long-standing and valuable history, the forest area has been designated a National Historic Site{{Sfn|Oisonomori (Heibonsha)|1991}}Explanation board for Oisonomori within the precincts.. File:Oiso-jinja, haiden.jpg|Worship hall File:Oiso-jinja, kaguraden.jpg|Kagura hall File:Oiso-jinja, enkei.jpg|Shrine grove (Oisonomori) seen from the former {{jalink|Nakasendō|Q1154479}} === Subsidiary shrines === * Suwa Shrine (Tangible Cultural Property designated by Ōmihachiman City) *: Enshrined deities: {{jalink|Takeminakata|Q9150391|Takeminakata-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Yasakatome|Q9150392|Yasakatome-no-Mikoto}}. Located to the west of the main hall. Although the exact construction date is unknown, it is thought to have been built around the Azuchi–Momoyama period. It is a one-bay {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} structure with a cypress-bark roof. Repairs were carried out in the 7th year of {{jalink|Ansei|Q1327259}} ({{jalink|1860|Q7691}}) and in {{jalink|1880|Q7806}} (Meiji 13), and it is regarded as a building that retains much of its original materials in good conditionExplanation board for the Suwa Shrine main hall within the Oiso Shrine precincts.. * Yoshizumi Inari Shrine File:Oiso-jinja, Yoshizumi-Inarisha.jpg|Yoshizumi Inari Shrine === Cultural properties === === Important Cultural Properties === * Main hall (structure) – Built in the Azuchi–Momoyama period, Tenshō 9 ({{jalink|1581|Q6717}}). Designated on April 17, {{jalink|1902|Q2043}} (Meiji 35){{National Cultural Properties Database|102|1474|Oiso Shrine Main Hall}}. === National Historic Sites === * Oisonomori – Designated on July 13, {{jalink|1949|Q5188}} ({{jalink|Shōwa|Q327567}} 24){{National Cultural Properties Database|401|1590|Oisonomori}}. === Cultural properties designated by Ōmihachiman City === * Tangible Cultural Properties ** Suwa Shrine main hall (structure) – Built around the Azuchi–Momoyama period. === Appearances in literature === * From the *{{jalink|Goshūi Wakashū}}*, a poem composed by Ōe no Kinsuke, Governor of Sagami, upon departing for his post in the first year of {{jalink|Manju|Q1194155}} ({{jalink|1024|Q19265}}) {{Cquote|On the eastern road,
I shall keep it as a memory—
the cuckoo,
one cry in the deep night
of '''Oisonomori'''|20px||*{{jalink|Goshūi Wakashū}}* {{Sfn|Oisonomori (Heibonsha)|1991}}}} === On-site information === '''Address''' * Higashi-Oiso 1615, {{jalink|Azuchi Town|Q165676}}, {{jalink|Ōmihachiman City|Q385461}}, {{jalink|Shiga Prefecture|Q131358}} '''Access''' * About 15 minutes by {{jalink|Akakon Bus|Q17190918}} from {{jalink|Azuchi Station|Q649689}} on the {{jalink|JR West|Q502125|JR}} {{jalink|Tōkaidō Main Line|Q1190152}} ({{jalink|Biwako Line|Q804135}}); get off at the Oiso Shrine stop. === Notes === {{Help notes}} {{Reflist|2}} === References === * On-site keidai.jpg|270px]]
Shrine precincts |Location={{ill|lt=Shiga Prefecture|Q131358|WD=Q131358}}{{ill|lt=Ōmihachiman City|Q385461|WD=Q385461}}{{ill|lt=Azuchi Town|Q165676|WD=Q165676}} Higashi-Oiso 1615 |Coordinates= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Deity={{ill|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|Q2564508|WD=Q2564508|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto}} |Shintai= |Shrine rank={{ill||lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}} (minor)
Former {{ill||lt=Prefectural Shrine|UNKNOWN}} |Founded=Traditionally: Year 5 of {{ill|lt=Emperor Kōrei|Q313041|WD=Q313041}} |Main hall=Three-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} |Alternate name=Kama-no-miya Shrine |Pilgrimage= |Annual festival=An auspicious day in April |Rituals= |Map= {{Location map|Japan Shiga|label=Oiso Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=250}} }} [[File:Oiso-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii gate}}]] '''Oiso Shrine''' (おいそじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in Higashi-Oiso, {{ill|lt=Azuchi Town|Q165676|WD=Q165676}}, {{ill|lt=Ōmihachiman City|Q385461|WD=Q385461}}, {{ill|lt=Shiga Prefecture|Q131358|WD=Q131358}}. It is a {{ill||lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}}, and under the {{ill|lt=modern system of shrine rankings|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former ranking}} it was a {{ill||lt=Prefectural Shrine|UNKNOWN}}. It is enshrined within Oisonomori (老蘇森), a nationally designated {{ill|lt=Historic Site|Q1081138|WD=Q1081138}}, along the {{ill|lt=Nakasendō|Q1154479|WD=Q1154479}} (also called the Nakasendō), north of the Higashi-Oiso settlement. === Enshrined deity === The currently enshrined deity is the following single kamiExplanation board within the precincts.. * {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|Q2564508|WD=Q2564508|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto}} The various deities enshrined in the main hall are hearth-fire prevention deities. Because the character for “kama” (sickle) was applied to “kamado” (hearth), the shrine came to be called “Kama Daimyōjin” or “Kama-no-miya.” Even today, parishioners maintain the custom of offering sickles to the shrine{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. === History === === Foundation === According to the *Oiso Shrine Honki* (colophon dated September of the first year of {{ill|lt=Shitoku (Japan)|Q1146202|WD=Q1146202|lt=Shitoku}} ({{ill|lt=1384|Q6347|WD=Q6347}})), the shrine began in the 5th year of the 7th emperor, {{ill|lt=Emperor Kōrei|Q313041|WD=Q313041}}, when Ishibe no Ōmuraji built a sacred altar in Oisonomori and enshrined the child deity of Ōtoshi-no-Kami{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. It further states that during the reign of the 10th emperor, {{ill|lt=Emperor Sujin|Q333530|WD=Q333530}}, {{ill||lt=Kibitsuhiko|UNKNOWN}} established a palace and enshrined Okutsuhiko-no-Mikoto and Okutsuhime-no-Mikoto, and that during the reign of the 15th emperor, {{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997}}, Kasa no Ason (a descendant of Kibitsuhiko) additionally enshrined {{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru}} and {{ill|lt=Oto Tachibana-hime|Q11487396|WD=Q11487396|lt=Tachibana-hime}}{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. One theory holds that the shrine originated in primitive mountain worship with Mount Kinugasa as the {{ill|lt=shintai-zan|Q1595289|WD=Q1595289}} (sacred mountain), and that the present site is a village shrine from which the {{ill|lt=iwakura|Q11587880|WD=Q11587880}} at the mountain summit is worshiped from afar{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. === Overview === In the *{{ill|lt=Shinshō Kakuchōfushō|Q11502312|WD=Q11502312}}* record dated the first year of {{ill|lt=Daido (Japan)|Q1373191|WD=Q1373191|lt=Daidō}} ({{ill|lt=806|Q30021|WD=Q30021}}), one household from Ōmi Province was assigned as {{ill|lt=shinko (household)|Q11589711|WD=Q11589711}} to the then “Oiso Deity”{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The *Oiso Shrine Honki* also records that in the first year of {{ill|lt=Ninju|Q1373077|WD=Q1373077}} ({{ill|lt=851|Q29966|WD=Q29966}}), the shrine was granted the divine rank of Senior Sixth Rank Upper{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. In the *{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}}* {{ill|lt=Register of Deities|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}} compiled in the 5th year of {{ill|lt=Enchō|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Enchō}} ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470}}), it is listed as “Oiso Shrine” in {{ill|lt=Gamo District|Q931658|WD=Q931658}}, {{ill|lt=Ōmi Province|Q843882|WD=Q843882}}, and is ranked as a {{ill||lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}}{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. In the 9th year of {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} ({{ill|lt=1581|Q6717|WD=Q6717}}), the main hall was rebuilt. In the {{ill|lt=early modern period|Q5308718|WD=Q5308718}}, it was known by names such as “Kama Daimyōjin” and “Kama-no-miya Shrine”{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. After the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707}}, under the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534}}, it was designated a village shrine in October {{ill|lt=1876|Q7766|WD=Q7766}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 9){{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. It was promoted to a township shrine in February {{ill|lt=1881|Q7808|WD=Q7808}} (Meiji 14), designated a shrine receiving state offerings in April {{ill|lt=1908|Q2056|WD=Q2056}} (Meiji 41), and in February {{ill|lt=1924|Q2173|WD=Q2173}} ({{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174}} 13) promoted to a {{ill||lt=Prefectural Shrine|UNKNOWN}} and renamed its current name, “Oiso Shrine” [http://www.shiga-jinjacho.jp/ycBBS/Board.cgi/02_jinja_db/db/ycDB_02jinja-pc-detail.html?mode:view=1&view:oid=653 Oiso Shrine] (Shiga Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines).. During the {{ill|lt=period of rapid economic growth|Q1135329|WD=Q1135329}}, parts of the precincts were taken for the {{ill|lt=National Route 8|Q640239|WD=Q640239}} bypass and the {{ill|lt=Tōkaidō Shinkansen|Q660895|WD=Q660895}}, resulting in the precincts being divided. === Precincts === [[File:Oiso-jinja, honden.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Main hall (right, a National {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622}})

The shrine grove surrounding the buildings is called “Oisonomori.” The present forest consists mainly of {{ill|lt=cedar|Q147388|WD=Q147388}}, {{ill|lt=cypress|Q917187|WD=Q917187}}, and {{ill|lt=pine|Q12024|WD=Q12024}}. According to tradition in the *Oiso Shrine Honki*, the land was once split open with water welling up and was uninhabitable, but when Ishibe no Ōmuraji planted saplings and prayed to the gods, it became a great forest; Ishibe no Ōmuraji lived to over a hundred years, and thus it came to be called the “Oiso (Old and Revived) Forest.” The name appears in a poem from the first year of {{ill|lt=Manju|Q1194155|WD=Q1194155}} ({{ill|lt=1024|Q19265|WD=Q19265}}) in the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292}}, and it is also introduced as a {{ill|lt=utamakura|Q1588579|WD=Q1588579}} of Ōmi Province in works such as the *{{ill|lt=Ryōjin Hishō|Q3010584|WD=Q3010584}}*, *Nōin Utamakura*, and *Waka Shogakushō*. Because it lay along the medieval {{ill|lt=Tōkaidō|Q1196306|WD=Q1196306}} and the early modern Nakasendō, it is also mentioned in travelogues from the {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|WD=Q236205}} onward, such as the *{{ill|lt=Tōkan Kikō|Q11528660|WD=Q11528660}}*. For these reasons, as a place of long-standing and valuable history, the forest area has been designated a National Historic Site{{Sfn|Oisonomori (Heibonsha)|1991}}Explanation board for Oisonomori within the precincts.. File:Oiso-jinja, haiden.jpg|Worship hall File:Oiso-jinja, kaguraden.jpg|Kagura hall File:Oiso-jinja, enkei.jpg|Shrine grove (Oisonomori) seen from the former {{ill|lt=Nakasendō|Q1154479|WD=Q1154479}} === Subsidiary shrines === * Suwa Shrine (Tangible Cultural Property designated by Ōmihachiman City) *: Enshrined deities: {{ill|lt=Takeminakata|Q9150391|WD=Q9150391|lt=Takeminakata-no-Mikoto}}, {{ill|lt=Yasakatome|Q9150392|WD=Q9150392|lt=Yasakatome-no-Mikoto}}. Located to the west of the main hall. Although the exact construction date is unknown, it is thought to have been built around the Azuchi–Momoyama period. It is a one-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} structure with a cypress-bark roof. Repairs were carried out in the 7th year of {{ill|lt=Ansei|Q1327259|WD=Q1327259}} ({{ill|lt=1860|Q7691|WD=Q7691}}) and in {{ill|lt=1880|Q7806|WD=Q7806}} (Meiji 13), and it is regarded as a building that retains much of its original materials in good conditionExplanation board for the Suwa Shrine main hall within the Oiso Shrine precincts.. * Yoshizumi Inari Shrine File:Oiso-jinja, Yoshizumi-Inarisha.jpg|Yoshizumi Inari Shrine === Cultural properties === === Important Cultural Properties === * Main hall (structure) – Built in the Azuchi–Momoyama period, Tenshō 9 ({{ill|lt=1581|Q6717|WD=Q6717}}). Designated on April 17, {{ill|lt=1902|Q2043|WD=Q2043}} (Meiji 35){{National Cultural Properties Database|102|1474|Oiso Shrine Main Hall}}. === National Historic Sites === * Oisonomori – Designated on July 13, {{ill|lt=1949|Q5188|WD=Q5188}} ({{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567}} 24){{National Cultural Properties Database|401|1590|Oisonomori}}. === Cultural properties designated by Ōmihachiman City === * Tangible Cultural Properties ** Suwa Shrine main hall (structure) – Built around the Azuchi–Momoyama period. === Appearances in literature === * From the *{{ill||lt=Goshūi Wakashū|UNKNOWN}}*, a poem composed by Ōe no Kinsuke, Governor of Sagami, upon departing for his post in the first year of {{ill|lt=Manju|Q1194155|WD=Q1194155}} ({{ill|lt=1024|Q19265|WD=Q19265}}) {{Cquote|On the eastern road,
I shall keep it as a memory—
the cuckoo,
one cry in the deep night
of '''Oisonomori'''|20px||*{{ill||lt=Goshūi Wakashū|UNKNOWN}}* {{Sfn|Oisonomori (Heibonsha)|1991}}}} === On-site information === '''Address''' * Higashi-Oiso 1615, {{ill|lt=Azuchi 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Oiso-jinja}} [[EN:Oiso Shrine]] [[JA:奥石神社]] {{wikidata link|Q24864985}} == Japanese content == {{Shinto shrine |Name=Oiso Shrine |Image=[[File:Oiso-jinja, keidai.jpg|270px]]
Shrine precincts |Location={{ill|lt=Shiga Prefecture|Q131358|WD=Q131358}}{{ill|lt=Ōmihachiman City|Q385461|WD=Q385461}}{{ill|lt=Azuchi Town|Q165676|WD=Q165676}} Higashi-Oiso 1615 |Coordinates= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Deity={{ill|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|Q2564508|WD=Q2564508|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto}} |Shintai= |Shrine rank={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}} (minor)
Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|UNKNOWN}} |Founded=Traditionally: Year 5 of {{ill|lt=Emperor Kōrei|Q313041|WD=Q313041}} |Main hall=Three-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} |Alternate name=Kama-no-miya Shrine |Pilgrimage= |Annual festival=An auspicious day in April |Rituals= |Map= {{Location map|Japan Shiga|label=Oiso Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=250}} }} [[File:Oiso-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii gate}}]] '''Oiso Shrine''' (おいそじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in Higashi-Oiso, {{ill|lt=Azuchi Town|Q165676|WD=Q165676}}, {{ill|lt=Ōmihachiman City|Q385461|WD=Q385461}}, {{ill|lt=Shiga Prefecture|Q131358|WD=Q131358}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}}, and under the {{ill|lt=modern system of shrine rankings|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former ranking}} it was a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|UNKNOWN}}. It is enshrined within Oisonomori (老蘇森), a nationally designated {{ill|lt=Historic Site|Q1081138|WD=Q1081138}}, along the {{ill|lt=Nakasendō|Q1154479|WD=Q1154479}} (also called the Nakasendō), north of the Higashi-Oiso settlement. === Enshrined deity === The currently enshrined deity is the following single kamiExplanation board within the precincts.. * {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|Q2564508|WD=Q2564508|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto}} The various deities enshrined in the main hall are hearth-fire prevention deities. Because the character for “kama” (sickle) was applied to “kamado” (hearth), the shrine came to be called “Kama Daimyōjin” or “Kama-no-miya.” Even today, parishioners maintain the custom of offering sickles to the shrine{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. === History === === Foundation === According to the *Oiso Shrine Honki* (colophon dated September of the first year of {{ill|lt=Shitoku (Japan)|Q1146202|WD=Q1146202|lt=Shitoku}} ({{ill|lt=1384|Q6347|WD=Q6347}})), the shrine began in the 5th year of the 7th emperor, {{ill|lt=Emperor Kōrei|Q313041|WD=Q313041}}, when Ishibe no Ōmuraji built a sacred altar in Oisonomori and enshrined the child deity of Ōtoshi-no-Kami{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. It further states that during the reign of the 10th emperor, {{ill|lt=Emperor Sujin|Q333530|WD=Q333530}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kibitsuhiko|UNKNOWN}} established a palace and enshrined Okutsuhiko-no-Mikoto and Okutsuhime-no-Mikoto, and that during the reign of the 15th emperor, {{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997}}, Kasa no Ason (a descendant of Kibitsuhiko) additionally enshrined {{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru}} and {{ill|lt=Oto Tachibana-hime|Q11487396|WD=Q11487396|lt=Tachibana-hime}}{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. One theory holds that the shrine originated in primitive mountain worship with Mount Kinugasa as the {{ill|lt=shintai-zan|Q1595289|WD=Q1595289}} (sacred mountain), and that the present site is a village shrine from which the {{ill|lt=iwakura|Q11587880|WD=Q11587880}} at the mountain summit is worshiped from afar{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. === Overview === In the *{{ill|lt=Shinshō Kakuchōfushō|Q11502312|WD=Q11502312}}* record dated the first year of {{ill|lt=Daido (Japan)|Q1373191|WD=Q1373191|lt=Daidō}} ({{ill|lt=806|Q30021|WD=Q30021}}), one household from Ōmi Province was assigned as {{ill|lt=shinko (household)|Q11589711|WD=Q11589711}} to the then “Oiso Deity”{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The *Oiso Shrine Honki* also records that in the first year of {{ill|lt=Ninju|Q1373077|WD=Q1373077}} ({{ill|lt=851|Q29966|WD=Q29966}}), the shrine was granted the divine rank of Senior Sixth Rank Upper{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. In the *{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}}* {{ill|lt=Register of Deities|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}} compiled in the 5th year of {{ill|lt=Enchō|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Enchō}} ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470}}), it is listed as “Oiso Shrine” in {{ill|lt=Gamo District|Q931658|WD=Q931658}}, {{ill|lt=Ōmi Province|Q843882|WD=Q843882}}, and is ranked as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}}{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. In the 9th year of {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} ({{ill|lt=1581|Q6717|WD=Q6717}}), the main hall was rebuilt. In the {{ill|lt=early modern period|Q5308718|WD=Q5308718}}, it was known by names such as “Kama Daimyōjin” and “Kama-no-miya Shrine”{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. After the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707}}, under the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534}}, it was designated a village shrine in October {{ill|lt=1876|Q7766|WD=Q7766}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 9){{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. It was promoted to a township shrine in February {{ill|lt=1881|Q7808|WD=Q7808}} (Meiji 14), designated a shrine receiving state offerings in April {{ill|lt=1908|Q2056|WD=Q2056}} (Meiji 41), and in February {{ill|lt=1924|Q2173|WD=Q2173}} ({{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174}} 13) promoted to a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|UNKNOWN}} and renamed its current name, “Oiso Shrine” [http://www.shiga-jinjacho.jp/ycBBS/Board.cgi/02_jinja_db/db/ycDB_02jinja-pc-detail.html?mode:view=1&view:oid=653 Oiso Shrine] (Shiga Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines).. During the {{ill|lt=period of rapid economic growth|Q1135329|WD=Q1135329}}, parts of the precincts were taken for the {{ill|lt=National Route 8|Q640239|WD=Q640239}} bypass and the {{ill|lt=Tōkaidō Shinkansen|Q660895|WD=Q660895}}, resulting in the precincts being divided. === Precincts === [[File:Oiso-jinja, honden.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Main hall (right, a National {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622}})
Suwa Shrine (left, a cultural property designated by Ōmihachiman City)}}]] [[File:Oiso-jinja, sandou.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Approach and shrine grove (Oisonomori)}}]] * Main hall ({{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622}}) – Rebuilt in the 9th year of {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} ({{ill|lt=1581|Q6717|WD=Q6717}}). It is a three-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} structure with a one-bay front porch and a cypress-bark roof. It features splendid decorations such as openwork arabesque carvings on the {{ill|lt=kamoi brackets|Q123117911|WD=Q123117911}}, carvings on the {{ill|lt=tesaki|Q124660898|WD=Q124660898}}, and latticework panels around the lower walls of the core. According to the {{ill|lt=ridge tag|Q8008834|WD=Q8008834}}, it was constructed by Shibata Iehisa, and a connection has been suggested with policies related to the formation of the castle town of {{ill|lt=Azuchi Castle|Q786888|WD=Q786888}} under {{ill|lt=Oda Nobunaga|Q171411|WD=Q171411}}Explanation board for the Oiso Shrine main hall within the precincts.. * Middle gate * Worship hall * Kagura hall * Shrine office === Oisonomori === A nationally designated {{ill|lt=Historic Site|Q1081138|WD=Q1081138}}. The shrine grove surrounding the buildings is called “Oisonomori.” The present forest consists mainly of {{ill|lt=cedar|Q147388|WD=Q147388}}, {{ill|lt=cypress|Q917187|WD=Q917187}}, and {{ill|lt=pine|Q12024|WD=Q12024}}. According to tradition in the *Oiso Shrine Honki*, the land was once split open with water welling up and was uninhabitable, but when Ishibe no Ōmuraji planted saplings and prayed to the gods, it became a great forest; Ishibe no Ōmuraji lived to over a hundred years, and thus it came to be called the “Oiso (Old and Revived) Forest.” The name appears in a poem from the first year of {{ill|lt=Manju|Q1194155|WD=Q1194155}} ({{ill|lt=1024|Q19265|WD=Q19265}}) in the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292}}, and it is also introduced as a {{ill|lt=utamakura|Q1588579|WD=Q1588579}} of Ōmi Province in works such as the *{{ill|lt=Ryōjin Hishō|Q3010584|WD=Q3010584}}*, *Nōin Utamakura*, and *Waka Shogakushō*. Because it lay along the medieval {{ill|lt=Tōkaidō|Q1196306|WD=Q1196306}} and the early modern Nakasendō, it is also mentioned in travelogues from the {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|WD=Q236205}} onward, such as the *{{ill|lt=Tōkan Kikō|Q11528660|WD=Q11528660}}*. For these reasons, as a place of long-standing and valuable history, the forest area has been designated a National Historic Site{{Sfn|Oisonomori (Heibonsha)|1991}}Explanation board for Oisonomori within the precincts.. Located to the west of the main hall. Although the exact construction date is unknown, it is thought to have been built around the Azuchi–Momoyama period. It is a one-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} structure with a cypress-bark roof. Repairs were carried out in the 7th year of {{ill|lt=Ansei|Q1327259|WD=Q1327259}} ({{ill|lt=1860|Q7691|WD=Q7691}}) and in {{ill|lt=1880|Q7806|WD=Q7806}} (Meiji 13), and it is regarded as a building that retains much of its original materials in good conditionExplanation board for the Suwa Shrine main hall within the Oiso Shrine precincts.. * Yoshizumi Inari Shrine File:Oiso-jinja, Yoshizumi-Inarisha.jpg|Yoshizumi Inari Shrine === Cultural properties === === Important Cultural Properties === * Main hall (structure) – Built in the Azuchi–Momoyama period, Tenshō 9 ({{ill|lt=1581|Q6717|WD=Q6717}}). Designated on April 17, {{ill|lt=1902|Q2043|WD=Q2043}} (Meiji 35){{National Cultural Properties Database|102|1474|Oiso Shrine Main Hall}}. === National Historic Sites === * Oisonomori – Designated on July 13, {{ill|lt=1949|Q5188|WD=Q5188}} ({{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567}} 24){{National Cultural Properties Database|401|1590|Oisonomori}}. === Cultural properties designated by Ōmihachiman City === * Tangible Cultural Properties ** Suwa Shrine main hall (structure) – Built around the Azuchi–Momoyama period. === Appearances in literature === * From the *{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Goshūi Wakashū|UNKNOWN}}*, a poem composed by Ōe no Kinsuke, Governor of Sagami, upon departing for his post in the first year of {{ill|lt=Manju|Q1194155|WD=Q1194155}} ({{ill|lt=1024|Q19265|WD=Q19265}}) {{Cquote|On the eastern road,

'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Oiso-jinja}} [[EN:Oiso Shrine]] [[JA:奥石神社]] {{wikidata link|Q24864985}} == Japanese content == {{Shinto shrine |Name=Oiso Shrine |Image=[[File:Oiso-jinja, keidai.jpg|270px]]
Shrine precincts |Location={{ill|lt=Shiga Prefecture|Q131358|WD=Q131358}}{{ill|lt=Ōmihachiman City|Q385461|WD=Q385461}}{{ill|lt=Azuchi Town|Q165676|WD=Q165676}} Higashi-Oiso 1615 |Coordinates= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Deity={{ill|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|Q2564508|WD=Q2564508|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto}} |Shintai= |Shrine rank={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN}} (minor)
Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} |Founded=Traditionally: Year 5 of {{ill|lt=Emperor Kōrei|Q313041|WD=Q313041}} |Main hall=Three-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} |Alternate name=Kama-no-miya Shrine |Pilgrimage= |Annual festival=An auspicious day in April |Rituals= |Map= {{Location map|Japan Shiga|label=Oiso Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=250}} }} [[File:Oiso-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii gate}}]] '''Oiso Shrine''' (おいそじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in Higashi-Oiso, {{ill|lt=Azuchi Town|Q165676|WD=Q165676}}, {{ill|lt=Ōmihachiman City|Q385461|WD=Q385461}}, {{ill|lt=Shiga Prefecture|Q131358|WD=Q131358}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN}}, and under the {{ill|lt=modern system of shrine rankings|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former ranking}} it was a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. It is enshrined within Oisonomori (老蘇森), a nationally designated {{ill|lt=Historic Site|Q1081138|WD=Q1081138}}, along the {{ill|lt=Nakasendō|Q1154479|WD=Q1154479}} (also called the Nakasendō), north of the Higashi-Oiso settlement. === Enshrined deity === The currently enshrined deity is the following single kamiExplanation board within the precincts.. * {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|Q2564508|WD=Q2564508|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto}} The various deities enshrined in the main hall are hearth-fire prevention deities. Because the character for “kama” (sickle) was applied to “kamado” (hearth), the shrine came to be called “Kama Daimyōjin” or “Kama-no-miya.” Even today, parishioners maintain the custom of offering sickles to the shrine{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. === History === === Foundation === According to the *Oiso Shrine Honki* (colophon dated September of the first year of {{ill|lt=Shitoku (Japan)|Q1146202|WD=Q1146202|lt=Shitoku}} ({{ill|lt=1384|Q6347|WD=Q6347}})), the shrine began in the 5th year of the 7th emperor, {{ill|lt=Emperor Kōrei|Q313041|WD=Q313041}}, when Ishibe no Ōmuraji built a sacred altar in Oisonomori and enshrined the child deity of Ōtoshi-no-Kami{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. It further states that during the reign of the 10th emperor, {{ill|lt=Emperor Sujin|Q333530|WD=Q333530}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kibitsuhiko|1=UNKNOWN}} established a palace and enshrined Okutsuhiko-no-Mikoto and Okutsuhime-no-Mikoto, and that during the reign of the 15th emperor, {{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997}}, Kasa no Ason (a descendant of Kibitsuhiko) additionally enshrined {{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru}} and {{ill|lt=Oto Tachibana-hime|Q11487396|WD=Q11487396|lt=Tachibana-hime}}{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. One theory holds that the shrine originated in primitive mountain worship with Mount Kinugasa as the {{ill|lt=shintai-zan|Q1595289|WD=Q1595289}} (sacred mountain), and that the present site is a village shrine from which the {{ill|lt=iwakura|Q11587880|WD=Q11587880}} at the mountain summit is worshiped from afar{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. === Overview === In the *{{ill|lt=Shinshō Kakuchōfushō|Q11502312|WD=Q11502312}}* record dated the first year of {{ill|lt=Daido (Japan)|Q1373191|WD=Q1373191|lt=Daidō}} ({{ill|lt=806|Q30021|WD=Q30021}}), one household from Ōmi Province was assigned as {{ill|lt=shinko (household)|Q11589711|WD=Q11589711}} to the then “Oiso Deity”{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The *Oiso Shrine Honki* also records that in the first year of {{ill|lt=Ninju|Q1373077|WD=Q1373077}} ({{ill|lt=851|Q29966|WD=Q29966}}), the shrine was granted the divine rank of Senior Sixth Rank Upper{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. In the *{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}}* {{ill|lt=Register of Deities|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}} compiled in the 5th year of {{ill|lt=Enchō|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Enchō}} ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470}}), it is listed as “Oiso Shrine” in {{ill|lt=Gamo District|Q931658|WD=Q931658}}, {{ill|lt=Ōmi Province|Q843882|WD=Q843882}}, and is ranked as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN}}{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. In the 9th year of {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} ({{ill|lt=1581|Q6717|WD=Q6717}}), the main hall was rebuilt. In the {{ill|lt=early modern period|Q5308718|WD=Q5308718}}, it was known by names such as “Kama Daimyōjin” and “Kama-no-miya Shrine”{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. After the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707}}, under the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534}}, it was designated a village shrine in October {{ill|lt=1876|Q7766|WD=Q7766}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 9){{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. It was promoted to a township shrine in February {{ill|lt=1881|Q7808|WD=Q7808}} (Meiji 14), designated a shrine receiving state offerings in April {{ill|lt=1908|Q2056|WD=Q2056}} (Meiji 41), and in February {{ill|lt=1924|Q2173|WD=Q2173}} ({{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174}} 13) promoted to a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} and renamed its current name, “Oiso Shrine” [http://www.shiga-jinjacho.jp/ycBBS/Board.cgi/02_jinja_db/db/ycDB_02jinja-pc-detail.html?mode:view=1&view:oid=653 Oiso Shrine] (Shiga Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines).. During the {{ill|lt=period of rapid economic growth|Q1135329|WD=Q1135329}}, parts of the precincts were taken for the {{ill|lt=National Route 8|Q640239|WD=Q640239}} bypass and the {{ill|lt=Tōkaidō Shinkansen|Q660895|WD=Q660895}}, resulting in the precincts being divided. === Precincts === [[File:Oiso-jinja, honden.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Main hall (right, a National {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622}})
Suwa Shrine (left, a cultural property designated by Ōmihachiman City)}}]] [[File:Oiso-jinja, sandou.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Approach and shrine grove (Oisonomori)}}]] * Main hall ({{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622}}) – Rebuilt in the 9th year of {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} ({{ill|lt=1581|Q6717|WD=Q6717}}). It is a three-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} structure with a one-bay front porch and a cypress-bark roof. It features splendid decorations such as openwork arabesque carvings on the {{ill|lt=kamoi brackets|Q123117911|WD=Q123117911}}, carvings on the {{ill|lt=tesaki|Q124660898|WD=Q124660898}}, and latticework panels around the lower walls of the core. According to the {{ill|lt=ridge tag|Q8008834|WD=Q8008834}}, it was constructed by Shibata Iehisa, and a connection has been suggested with policies related to the formation of the castle town of {{ill|lt=Azuchi Castle|Q786888|WD=Q786888}} under {{ill|lt=Oda Nobunaga|Q171411|WD=Q171411}}Explanation board for the Oiso Shrine main hall within the precincts.. * Middle gate * Worship hall * Kagura hall * Shrine office === Oisonomori === A nationally designated {{ill|lt=Historic Site|Q1081138|WD=Q1081138}}. The shrine grove surrounding the buildings is called “Oisonomori.” The present forest consists mainly of {{ill|lt=cedar|Q147388|WD=Q147388}}, {{ill|lt=cypress|Q917187|WD=Q917187}}, and {{ill|lt=pine|Q12024|WD=Q12024}}. According to tradition in the *Oiso Shrine Honki*, the land was once split open with water welling up and was uninhabitable, but when Ishibe no Ōmuraji planted saplings and prayed to the gods, it became a great forest; Ishibe no Ōmuraji lived to over a hundred years, and thus it came to be called the “Oiso (Old and Revived) Forest.” The name appears in a poem from the first year of {{ill|lt=Manju|Q1194155|WD=Q1194155}} ({{ill|lt=1024|Q19265|WD=Q19265}}) in the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292}}, and it is also introduced as a {{ill|lt=utamakura|Q1588579|WD=Q1588579}} of Ōmi Province in works such as the *{{ill|lt=Ryōjin Hishō|Q3010584|WD=Q3010584}}*, *Nōin Utamakura*, and *Waka Shogakushō*. Because it lay along the medieval {{ill|lt=Tōkaidō|Q1196306|WD=Q1196306}} and the early modern Nakasendō, it is also mentioned in travelogues from the {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|WD=Q236205}} onward, such as the *{{ill|lt=Tōkan Kikō|Q11528660|WD=Q11528660}}*. For these reasons, as a place of long-standing and valuable history, the forest area has been designated a National Historic Site{{Sfn|Oisonomori (Heibonsha)|1991}}Explanation board for Oisonomori within the precincts.. File:Oiso-jinja, haiden.jpg|Worship hall File:Oiso-jinja, kaguraden.jpg|Kagura hall File:Oiso-jinja, enkei.jpg|Shrine grove (Oisonomori) seen from the former {{ill|lt=Nakasendō|Q1154479|WD=Q1154479}} === Subsidiary shrines === * Suwa Shrine (Tangible Cultural Property designated by Ōmihachiman City) *: Enshrined deities: {{ill|lt=Takeminakata|Q9150391|WD=Q9150391|lt=Takeminakata-no-Mikoto}}, {{ill|lt=Yasakatome|Q9150392|WD=Q9150392|lt=Yasakatome-no-Mikoto}}. Located to the west of the main hall. Although the exact construction date is unknown, it is thought to have been built around the Azuchi–Momoyama period. It is a one-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} structure with a cypress-bark roof. Repairs were carried out in the 7th year of {{ill|lt=Ansei|Q1327259|WD=Q1327259}} ({{ill|lt=1860|Q7691|WD=Q7691}}) and in {{ill|lt=1880|Q7806|WD=Q7806}} (Meiji 13), and it is regarded as a building that retains much of its original materials in good conditionExplanation board for the Suwa Shrine main hall within the Oiso Shrine precincts.. * Yoshizumi Inari Shrine File:Oiso-jinja, Yoshizumi-Inarisha.jpg|Yoshizumi Inari Shrine === Cultural properties === === Important Cultural Properties === * Main hall (structure) – Built in the Azuchi–Momoyama period, Tenshō 9 ({{ill|lt=1581|Q6717|WD=Q6717}}).
