'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Oiso-jinja}} [[EN:Oiso Shrine]] [[JA:奥石神社]] {{wikidata link|Q24864985}} == Japanese content == {{Shinto shrine |Name=Oiso Shrine |Image=[[File:Oiso-jinja, keidai.jpg|270px]]
Shrine precincts |Location={{ill|lt=Shiga Prefecture|Q131358|WD=Q131358}}{{ill|lt=Ōmihachiman City|Q385461|WD=Q385461}}{{ill|lt=Azuchi Town|Q165676|WD=Q165676}} Higashi-Oiso 1615 |Coordinates= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Deity={{ill|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|Q2564508|WD=Q2564508|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto}} |Shintai= |Shrine rank={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} (minor)
Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} |Founded=Traditionally: Year 5 of {{ill|lt=Emperor Kōrei|Q313041|WD=Q313041}} |Main hall=Three-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} |Alternate name=Kama-no-miya Shrine |Pilgrimage= |Annual festival=An auspicious day in April |Rituals= |Map= {{Location map|Japan Shiga|label=Oiso Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=250}} }} [[File:Oiso-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii gate}}]] '''Oiso Shrine''' (おいそじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in Higashi-Oiso, {{ill|lt=Azuchi Town|Q165676|WD=Q165676}}, {{ill|lt=Ōmihachiman City|Q385461|WD=Q385461}}, {{ill|lt=Shiga Prefecture|Q131358|WD=Q131358}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}}, and under the {{ill|lt=modern system of shrine rankings|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former ranking}} it was a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. It is enshrined within Oisonomori (老蘇森), a nationally designated {{ill|lt=Historic Site|Q1081138|WD=Q1081138}}, along the {{ill|lt=Nakasendō|Q1154479|WD=Q1154479}} (also called the Nakasendō), north of the Higashi-Oiso settlement. === Enshrined deity === The currently enshrined deity is the following single kamiExplanation board within the precincts.. * {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|Q2564508|WD=Q2564508|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto}} The various deities enshrined in the main hall are hearth-fire prevention deities. Because the character for “kama” (sickle) was applied to “kamado” (hearth), the shrine came to be called “Kama Daimyōjin” or “Kama-no-miya.” Even today, parishioners maintain the custom of offering sickles to the shrine{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. === History === === Foundation === According to the *Oiso Shrine Honki* (colophon dated September of the first year of {{ill|lt=Shitoku (Japan)|Q1146202|WD=Q1146202|lt=Shitoku}} ({{ill|lt=1384|Q6347|WD=Q6347}})), the shrine began in the 5th year of the 7th emperor, {{ill|lt=Emperor Kōrei|Q313041|WD=Q313041}}, when Ishibe no Ōmuraji built a sacred altar in Oisonomori and enshrined the child deity of Ōtoshi-no-Kami{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. It further states that during the reign of the 10th emperor, {{ill|lt=Emperor Sujin|Q333530|WD=Q333530}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kibitsuhiko|1=UNKNOWN}} established a palace and enshrined Okutsuhiko-no-Mikoto and Okutsuhime-no-Mikoto, and that during the reign of the 15th emperor, {{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997}}, Kasa no Ason (a descendant of Kibitsuhiko) additionally enshrined {{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru}} and {{ill|lt=Oto Tachibana-hime|Q11487396|WD=Q11487396|lt=Tachibana-hime}}{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. One theory holds that the shrine originated in primitive mountain worship with Mount Kinugasa as the {{ill|lt=shintai-zan|Q1595289|WD=Q1595289}} (sacred mountain), and that the present site is a village shrine from which the {{ill|lt=iwakura|Q11587880|WD=Q11587880}} at the mountain summit is worshiped from afar{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. === Overview === In the *{{ill|lt=Shinshō Kakuchōfushō|Q11502312|WD=Q11502312}}* record dated the first year of {{ill|lt=Daido (Japan)|Q1373191|WD=Q1373191|lt=Daidō}} ({{ill|lt=806|Q30021|WD=Q30021}}), one household from Ōmi Province was assigned as {{ill|lt=shinko (household)|Q11589711|WD=Q11589711}} to the then “Oiso Deity”{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The *Oiso Shrine Honki* also records that in the first year of {{ill|lt=Ninju|Q1373077|WD=Q1373077}} ({{ill|lt=851|Q29966|WD=Q29966}}), the shrine was granted the divine rank of Senior Sixth Rank Upper{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. In the *{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}}* {{ill|lt=Register of Deities|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}} compiled in the 5th year of {{ill|lt=Enchō|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Enchō}} ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470}}), it is listed as “Oiso Shrine” in {{ill|lt=Gamo District|Q931658|WD=Q931658}}, {{ill|lt=Ōmi Province|Q843882|WD=Q843882}}, and is ranked as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}}{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. In the 9th year of {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} ({{ill|lt=1581|Q6717|WD=Q6717}}), the main hall was rebuilt. In the {{ill|lt=early modern period|Q5308718|WD=Q5308718}}, it was known by names such as “Kama Daimyōjin” and “Kama-no-miya Shrine”{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. After the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707}}, under the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534}}, it was designated a village shrine in October {{ill|lt=1876|Q7766|WD=Q7766}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 9){{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. It was promoted to a township shrine in February {{ill|lt=1881|Q7808|WD=Q7808}} (Meiji 14), designated a shrine receiving state offerings in April {{ill|lt=1908|Q2056|WD=Q2056}} (Meiji 41), and in February {{ill|lt=1924|Q2173|WD=Q2173}} ({{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174}} 13) promoted to a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} and renamed its current name, “Oiso Shrine” [http://www.shiga-jinjacho.jp/ycBBS/Board.cgi/02_jinja_db/db/ycDB_02jinja-pc-detail.html?mode:view=1&view:oid=653 Oiso Shrine] (Shiga Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines).. During the {{ill|lt=period of rapid economic growth|Q1135329|WD=Q1135329}}, parts of the precincts were taken for the {{ill|lt=National Route 8|Q640239|WD=Q640239}} bypass and the {{ill|lt=Tōkaidō Shinkansen|Q660895|WD=Q660895}}, resulting in the precincts being divided. === Precincts === [[File:Oiso-jinja, honden.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Main hall (right, a National {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622}})
Suwa Shrine (left, a cultural property designated by Ōmihachiman City)}}]] [[File:Oiso-jinja, sandou.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Approach and shrine grove (Oisonomori)}}]] * Main hall ({{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622}}) – Rebuilt in the 9th year of {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} ({{ill|lt=1581|Q6717|WD=Q6717}}). It is a three-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} structure with a one-bay front porch and a cypress-bark roof. It features splendid decorations such as openwork arabesque carvings on the {{ill|lt=kamoi brackets|Q123117911|WD=Q123117911}}, carvings on the {{ill|lt=tesaki|Q124660898|WD=Q124660898}}, and latticework panels around the lower walls of the core. According to the {{ill|lt=ridge tag|Q8008834|WD=Q8008834}}, it was constructed by Shibata Iehisa, and a connection has been suggested with policies related to the formation of the castle town of {{ill|lt=Azuchi Castle|Q786888|WD=Q786888}} under {{ill|lt=Oda Nobunaga|Q171411|WD=Q171411}}Explanation board for the Oiso Shrine main hall within the precincts.. * Middle gate * Worship hall * Kagura hall * Shrine office === Oisonomori === A nationally designated {{ill|lt=Historic Site|Q1081138|WD=Q1081138}}. The shrine grove surrounding the buildings is called “Oisonomori.” The present forest consists mainly of {{ill|lt=cedar|Q147388|WD=Q147388}}, {{ill|lt=cypress|Q917187|WD=Q917187}}, and {{ill|lt=pine|Q12024|WD=Q12024}}. According to tradition in the *Oiso Shrine Honki*, the land was once split open with water welling up and was uninhabitable, but when Ishibe no Ōmuraji planted saplings and prayed to the gods, it became a great forest; Ishibe no Ōmuraji lived to over a hundred years, and thus it came to be called the “Oiso (Old and Revived) Forest.” The name appears in a poem from the first year of {{ill|lt=Manju|Q1194155|WD=Q1194155}} ({{ill|lt=1024|Q19265|WD=Q19265}}) in the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292}}, and it is also introduced as a {{ill|lt=utamakura|Q1588579|WD=Q1588579}} of Ōmi Province in works such as the *{{ill|lt=Ryōjin Hishō|Q3010584|WD=Q3010584}}*, *Nōin Utamakura*, and *Waka Shogakushō*. Because it lay along the medieval {{ill|lt=Tōkaidō|Q1196306|WD=Q1196306}} and the early modern Nakasendō, it is also mentioned in travelogues from the {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|WD=Q236205}} onward, such as the *{{ill|lt=Tōkan Kikō|Q11528660|WD=Q11528660}}*. For these reasons, as a place of long-standing and valuable history, the forest area has been designated a National Historic Site{{Sfn|Oisonomori (Heibonsha)|1991}}Explanation board for Oisonomori within the precincts.. File:Oiso-jinja, haiden.jpg|Worship hall File:Oiso-jinja, kaguraden.jpg|Kagura hall File:Oiso-jinja, enkei.jpg|Shrine grove (Oisonomori) seen from the former {{ill|lt=Nakasendō|Q1154479|WD=Q1154479}} === Subsidiary shrines === * Suwa Shrine (Tangible Cultural Property designated by Ōmihachiman City) *: Enshrined deities: {{ill|lt=Takeminakata|Q9150391|WD=Q9150391|lt=Takeminakata-no-Mikoto}}, {{ill|lt=Yasakatome|Q9150392|WD=Q9150392|lt=Yasakatome-no-Mikoto}}. Located to the west of the main hall. Although the exact construction date is unknown, it is thought to have been built around the Azuchi–Momoyama period. It is a one-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} structure with a cypress-bark roof. Repairs were carried out in the 7th year of {{ill|lt=Ansei|Q1327259|WD=Q1327259}} ({{ill|lt=1860|Q7691|WD=Q7691}}) and in {{ill|lt=1880|Q7806|WD=Q7806}} (Meiji 13), and it is regarded as a building that retains much of its original materials in good conditionExplanation board for the Suwa Shrine main hall within the Oiso Shrine precincts.. * Yoshizumi Inari Shrine File:Oiso-jinja, Yoshizumi-Inarisha.jpg|Yoshizumi Inari Shrine === Cultural properties === === Important Cultural Properties === * Main hall (structure) – Built in the Azuchi–Momoyama period, Tenshō 9 ({{ill|lt=1581|Q6717|WD=Q6717}}). Designated on April 17, {{ill|lt=1902|Q2043|WD=Q2043}} (Meiji 35){{National Cultural Properties Database|102|1474|Oiso Shrine Main Hall}}. === National Historic Sites === * Oisonomori – Designated on July 13, {{ill|lt=1949|Q5188|WD=Q5188}} ({{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567}} 24){{National Cultural Properties Database|401|1590|Oisonomori}}. === Cultural properties designated by Ōmihachiman City === * Tangible Cultural Properties ** Suwa Shrine main hall (structure) – Built around the Azuchi–Momoyama period. === Appearances in literature === * From the *{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Goshūi Wakashū|1=UNKNOWN}}*, a poem composed by Ōe no Kinsuke, Governor of Sagami, upon departing for his post in the first year of {{ill|lt=Manju|Q1194155|WD=Q1194155}} ({{ill|lt=1024|Q19265|WD=Q19265}}) {{Cquote|On the eastern road,
I shall keep it as a memory—
the cuckoo,
one cry in the deep night
of '''Oisonomori'''|20px||*{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Goshūi Wakashū|1=UNKNOWN}}* {{Sfn|Oisonomori (Heibonsha)|1991}}}} === On-site information === '''Address''' * Higashi-Oiso 1615, {{ill|lt=Azuchi Town|Q165676|WD=Q165676}}, {{ill|lt=Ōmihachiman City|Q385461|WD=Q385461}}, {{ill|lt=Shiga Prefecture|Q131358|WD=Q131358}} '''Access''' * About 15 minutes by {{ill|lt=Akakon Bus|Q17190918|WD=Q17190918}} from {{ill|lt=Azuchi Station|Q649689|WD=Q649689}} on the {{ill|lt=JR West|Q502125|WD=Q502125|lt=JR}} {{ill|lt=Tōkaidō Main Line|Q1190152|WD=Q1190152}} ({{ill|lt=Biwako 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Oiso-jinja}} [[EN:Oiso Shrine]] [[JA:奥石神社]] {{wikidata link|Q24864985}} == Japanese content == {{Shinto shrine |Name=Oiso Shrine |Image=[[File:Oiso-jinja, keidai.jpg|270px]]
Shrine precincts |Location={{ill|lt=Shiga Prefecture|Q131358|WD=Q131358}}{{ill|lt=Ōmihachiman City|Q385461|WD=Q385461}}{{ill|lt=Azuchi Town|Q165676|WD=Q165676}} Higashi-Oiso 1615 |Coordinates= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Deity={{ill|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|Q2564508|WD=Q2564508|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto}} |Shintai= |Shrine rank={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} (minor)
Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} |Founded=Traditionally: Year 5 of {{ill|lt=Emperor Kōrei|Q313041|WD=Q313041}} |Main hall=Three-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} |Alternate name=Kama-no-miya Shrine |Pilgrimage= |Annual festival=An auspicious day in April |Rituals= |Map= {{Location map|Japan Shiga|label=Oiso Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=250}} }} [[File:Oiso-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii gate}}]] '''Oiso Shrine''' (おいそじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in Higashi-Oiso, {{ill|lt=Azuchi Town|Q165676|WD=Q165676}}, {{ill|lt=Ōmihachiman City|Q385461|WD=Q385461}}, {{ill|lt=Shiga Prefecture|Q131358|WD=Q131358}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}}, and under the {{ill|lt=modern system of shrine rankings|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former ranking}} it was a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. It is enshrined within Oisonomori (老蘇森), a nationally designated {{ill|lt=Historic Site|Q1081138|WD=Q1081138}}, along the {{ill|lt=Nakasendō|Q1154479|WD=Q1154479}} (also called the Nakasendō), north of the Higashi-Oiso settlement. === Enshrined deity === The currently enshrined deity is the following single kamiExplanation board within the precincts.. * {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|Q2564508|WD=Q2564508|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto}} The various deities enshrined in the main hall are hearth-fire prevention deities. Because the character for “kama” (sickle) was applied to “kamado” (hearth), the shrine came to be called “Kama Daimyōjin” or “Kama-no-miya.” Even today, parishioners maintain the custom of offering sickles to the shrine{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. === History === === Foundation === According to the *Oiso Shrine Honki* (colophon dated September of the first year of {{ill|lt=Shitoku (Japan)|Q1146202|WD=Q1146202|lt=Shitoku}} ({{ill|lt=1384|Q6347|WD=Q6347}})), the shrine began in the 5th year of the 7th emperor, {{ill|lt=Emperor Kōrei|Q313041|WD=Q313041}}, when Ishibe no Ōmuraji built a sacred altar in Oisonomori and enshrined the child deity of Ōtoshi-no-Kami{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. It further states that during the reign of the 10th emperor, {{ill|lt=Emperor Sujin|Q333530|WD=Q333530}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kibitsuhiko|1=UNKNOWN}} established a palace and enshrined Okutsuhiko-no-Mikoto and Okutsuhime-no-Mikoto, and that during the reign of the 15th emperor, {{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997}}, Kasa no Ason (a descendant of Kibitsuhiko) additionally enshrined {{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru}} and {{ill|lt=Oto Tachibana-hime|Q11487396|WD=Q11487396|lt=Tachibana-hime}}{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. One theory holds that the shrine originated in primitive mountain worship with Mount Kinugasa as the {{ill|lt=shintai-zan|Q1595289|WD=Q1595289}} (sacred mountain), and that the present site is a village shrine from which the {{ill|lt=iwakura|Q11587880|WD=Q11587880}} at the mountain summit is worshiped from afar{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. === Overview === In the *{{ill|lt=Shinshō Kakuchōfushō|Q11502312|WD=Q11502312}}* record dated the first year of {{ill|lt=Daido (Japan)|Q1373191|WD=Q1373191|lt=Daidō}} ({{ill|lt=806|Q30021|WD=Q30021}}), one household from Ōmi Province was assigned as {{ill|lt=shinko (household)|Q11589711|WD=Q11589711}} to the then “Oiso Deity”{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The *Oiso Shrine Honki* also records that in the first year of {{ill|lt=Ninju|Q1373077|WD=Q1373077}} ({{ill|lt=851|Q29966|WD=Q29966}}), the shrine was granted the divine rank of Senior Sixth Rank Upper{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. In the *{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}}* {{ill|lt=Register of Deities|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}} compiled in the 5th year of {{ill|lt=Enchō|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Enchō}} ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470}}), it is listed as “Oiso Shrine” in {{ill|lt=Gamo District|Q931658|WD=Q931658}}, {{ill|lt=Ōmi Province|Q843882|WD=Q843882}}, and is ranked as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}}{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. In the 9th year of {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} ({{ill|lt=1581|Q6717|WD=Q6717}}), the main hall was rebuilt. In the {{ill|lt=early modern period|Q5308718|WD=Q5308718}}, it was known by names such as “Kama Daimyōjin” and “Kama-no-miya Shrine”{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. After the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707}}, under the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534}}, it was designated a village shrine in October {{ill|lt=1876|Q7766|WD=Q7766}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 9){{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. It was promoted to a township shrine in February {{ill|lt=1881|Q7808|WD=Q7808}} (Meiji 14), designated a shrine receiving state offerings in April {{ill|lt=1908|Q2056|WD=Q2056}} (Meiji 41), and in February {{ill|lt=1924|Q2173|WD=Q2173}} ({{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174}} 13) promoted to a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} and renamed its current name, “Oiso Shrine” [http://www.shiga-jinjacho.jp/ycBBS/Board.cgi/02_jinja_db/db/ycDB_02jinja-pc-detail.html?mode:view=1&view:oid=653 Oiso Shrine] (Shiga Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines).. During the {{ill|lt=period of rapid economic growth|Q1135329|WD=Q1135329}}, parts of the precincts were taken for the {{ill|lt=National Route 8|Q640239|WD=Q640239}} bypass and the {{ill|lt=Tōkaidō Shinkansen|Q660895|WD=Q660895}}, resulting in the precincts being divided. === Precincts === [[File:Oiso-jinja, honden.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Main hall (right, a National {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622}})
Suwa Shrine (left, a cultural property designated by Ōmihachiman City)}}]] [[File:Oiso-jinja, sandou.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Approach and shrine grove (Oisonomori)}}]] * Main hall ({{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622}}) – Rebuilt in the 9th year of {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} ({{ill|lt=1581|Q6717|WD=Q6717}}). It is a three-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} structure with a one-bay front porch and a cypress-bark roof. It features splendid decorations such as openwork arabesque carvings on the {{ill|lt=kamoi brackets|Q123117911|WD=Q123117911}}, carvings on the {{ill|lt=tesaki|Q124660898|WD=Q124660898}}, and latticework panels around the lower walls of the core. According to the {{ill|lt=ridge tag|Q8008834|WD=Q8008834}}, it was constructed by Shibata Iehisa, and a connection has been suggested with policies related to the formation of the castle town of {{ill|lt=Azuchi Castle|Q786888|WD=Q786888}} under {{ill|lt=Oda Nobunaga|Q171411|WD=Q171411}}Explanation board for the Oiso Shrine main hall within the precincts.. * Middle gate * Worship hall * Kagura hall * Shrine office === Oisonomori === A nationally designated {{ill|lt=Historic Site|Q1081138|WD=Q1081138}}. The shrine grove surrounding the buildings is called “Oisonomori.” The present forest consists mainly of {{ill|lt=cedar|Q147388|WD=Q147388}}, {{ill|lt=cypress|Q917187|WD=Q917187}}, and {{ill|lt=pine|Q12024|WD=Q12024}}. According to tradition in the *Oiso Shrine Honki*, the land was once split open with water welling up and was uninhabitable, but when Ishibe no Ōmuraji planted saplings and prayed to the gods, it became a great forest; Ishibe no Ōmuraji lived to over a hundred years, and thus it came to be called the “Oiso (Old and Revived) Forest.” The name appears in a poem from the first year of {{ill|lt=Manju|Q1194155|WD=Q1194155}} ({{ill|lt=1024|Q19265|WD=Q19265}}) in the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292}}, and it is also introduced as a {{ill|lt=utamakura|Q1588579|WD=Q1588579}} of Ōmi Province in works such as the *{{ill|lt=Ryōjin Hishō|Q3010584|WD=Q3010584}}*, *Nōin Utamakura*, and *Waka Shogakushō*. Because it lay along the medieval {{ill|lt=Tōkaidō|Q1196306|WD=Q1196306}} and the early modern Nakasendō, it is also mentioned in travelogues from the {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|WD=Q236205}} onward, such as the *{{ill|lt=Tōkan Kikō|Q11528660|WD=Q11528660}}*. For these reasons, as a place of long-standing and valuable history, the forest area has been designated a National Historic Site{{Sfn|Oisonomori (Heibonsha)|1991}}Explanation board for Oisonomori within the precincts.. File:Oiso-jinja, haiden.jpg|Worship hall File:Oiso-jinja, kaguraden.jpg|Kagura hall File:Oiso-jinja, enkei.jpg|Shrine grove (Oisonomori) seen from the former {{ill|lt=Nakasendō|Q1154479|WD=Q1154479}} === Subsidiary shrines === * Suwa Shrine (Tangible Cultural Property designated by Ōmihachiman City) *: Enshrined deities: {{ill|lt=Takeminakata|Q9150391|WD=Q9150391|lt=Takeminakata-no-Mikoto}}, {{ill|lt=Yasakatome|Q9150392|WD=Q9150392|lt=Yasakatome-no-Mikoto}}. Located to the west of the main hall. Although the exact construction date is unknown, it is thought to have been built around the Azuchi–Momoyama period. It is a one-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} structure with a cypress-bark roof. Repairs were carried out in the 7th year of {{ill|lt=Ansei|Q1327259|WD=Q1327259}} ({{ill|lt=1860|Q7691|WD=Q7691}}) and in {{ill|lt=1880|Q7806|WD=Q7806}} (Meiji 13), and it is regarded as a building that retains much of its original materials in good conditionExplanation board for the Suwa Shrine main hall within the Oiso Shrine precincts.. * Yoshizumi Inari Shrine File:Oiso-jinja, Yoshizumi-Inarisha.jpg|Yoshizumi Inari Shrine === Cultural properties === === Important Cultural Properties === * Main hall (structure) – Built in the Azuchi–Momoyama period, Tenshō 9 ({{ill|lt=1581|Q6717|WD=Q6717}}). Designated on April 17, {{ill|lt=1902|Q2043|WD=Q2043}} (Meiji 35){{National Cultural Properties Database|102|1474|Oiso Shrine Main Hall}}. === National Historic Sites === * Oisonomori – Designated on July 13, {{ill|lt=1949|Q5188|WD=Q5188}} ({{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567}} 24){{National Cultural Properties Database|401|1590|Oisonomori}}. === Cultural properties designated by Ōmihachiman City === * Tangible Cultural Properties ** Suwa Shrine main hall (structure) – Built around the Azuchi–Momoyama period. === Appearances in literature === * From the *{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Goshūi Wakashū|1=UNKNOWN}}*, a poem composed by Ōe no Kinsuke, Governor of Sagami, upon departing for his post in the first year of {{ill|lt=Manju|Q1194155|WD=Q1194155}} ({{ill|lt=1024|Q19265|WD=Q19265}}) {{Cquote|On the eastern road,
I shall keep it as a memory—
the cuckoo,
one cry in the deep night
of '''Oisonomori'''|20px||*{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Goshūi Wakashū|1=UNKNOWN}}* {{Sfn|Oisonomori (Heibonsha)|1991}}}} === On-site information === '''Address''' * Higashi-Oiso 1615, {{ill|lt=Azuchi Town|Q165676|WD=Q165676}}, {{ill|lt=Ōmihachiman City|Q385461|WD=Q385461}}, {{ill|lt=Shiga Prefecture|Q131358|WD=Q131358}} '''Access''' * About 15 minutes by {{ill|lt=Akakon Bus|Q17190918|WD=Q17190918}} from {{ill|lt=Azuchi Station|Q649689|WD=Q649689}} on the {{ill|lt=JR West|Q502125|WD=Q502125|lt=JR}} {{ill|lt=Tōkaidō Main Line|Q1190152|WD=Q1190152}} ({{ill|lt=Biwako 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Oiso-jinja}} [[EN:Oiso Shrine]] [[JA:奥石神社]] {{wikidata link|Q24864985}} == Japanese content == {{Shinto shrine |Name=Oiso Shrine |Image=[[File:Oiso-jinja, keidai.jpg|270px]]
Shrine precincts |Location={{ill|lt=Shiga Prefecture|Q131358|WD=Q131358}}{{ill|lt=Ōmihachiman City|Q385461|WD=Q385461}}{{ill|lt=Azuchi Town|Q165676|WD=Q165676}} Higashi-Oiso 1615 |Coordinates= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Deity={{ill|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|Q2564508|WD=Q2564508|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto}} |Shintai= |Shrine rank={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} (minor)
Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} |Founded=Traditionally: Year 5 of {{ill|lt=Emperor Kōrei|Q313041|WD=Q313041}} |Main hall=Three-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} |Alternate name=Kama-no-miya Shrine |Pilgrimage= |Annual festival=An auspicious day in April |Rituals= |Map= {{Location map|Japan Shiga|label=Oiso Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=250}} }} [[File:Oiso-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii gate}}]] '''Oiso Shrine''' (おいそじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in Higashi-Oiso, {{ill|lt=Azuchi Town|Q165676|WD=Q165676}}, {{ill|lt=Ōmihachiman City|Q385461|WD=Q385461}}, {{ill|lt=Shiga Prefecture|Q131358|WD=Q131358}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}}, and under the {{ill|lt=modern system of shrine rankings|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former ranking}} it was a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. It is enshrined within Oisonomori (老蘇森), a nationally designated {{ill|lt=Historic Site|Q1081138|WD=Q1081138}}, along the {{ill|lt=Nakasendō|Q1154479|WD=Q1154479}} (also called the Nakasendō), north of the Higashi-Oiso settlement. === Enshrined deity === The currently enshrined deity is the following single kamiExplanation board within the precincts.. * {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|Q2564508|WD=Q2564508|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto}} The various deities enshrined in the main hall are hearth-fire prevention deities. Because the character for “kama” (sickle) was applied to “kamado” (hearth), the shrine came to be called “Kama Daimyōjin” or “Kama-no-miya.” Even today, parishioners maintain the custom of offering sickles to the shrine{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. === History === === Foundation === According to the *Oiso Shrine Honki* (colophon dated September of the first year of {{ill|lt=Shitoku (Japan)|Q1146202|WD=Q1146202|lt=Shitoku}} ({{ill|lt=1384|Q6347|WD=Q6347}})), the shrine began in the 5th year of the 7th emperor, {{ill|lt=Emperor Kōrei|Q313041|WD=Q313041}}, when Ishibe no Ōmuraji built a sacred altar in Oisonomori and enshrined the child deity of Ōtoshi-no-Kami{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. It further states that during the reign of the 10th emperor, {{ill|lt=Emperor Sujin|Q333530|WD=Q333530}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kibitsuhiko|1=UNKNOWN}} established a palace and enshrined Okutsuhiko-no-Mikoto and Okutsuhime-no-Mikoto, and that during the reign of the 15th emperor, {{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997}}, Kasa no Ason (a descendant of Kibitsuhiko) additionally enshrined {{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru}} and {{ill|lt=Oto Tachibana-hime|Q11487396|WD=Q11487396|lt=Tachibana-hime}}{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. One theory holds that the shrine originated in primitive mountain worship with Mount Kinugasa as the {{ill|lt=shintai-zan|Q1595289|WD=Q1595289}} (sacred mountain), and that the present site is a village shrine from which the {{ill|lt=iwakura|Q11587880|WD=Q11587880}} at the mountain summit is worshiped from afar{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. === Overview === In the *{{ill|lt=Shinshō Kakuchōfushō|Q11502312|WD=Q11502312}}* record dated the first year of {{ill|lt=Daido (Japan)|Q1373191|WD=Q1373191|lt=Daidō}} ({{ill|lt=806|Q30021|WD=Q30021}}), one household from Ōmi Province was assigned as {{ill|lt=shinko (household)|Q11589711|WD=Q11589711}} to the then “Oiso Deity”{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The *Oiso Shrine Honki* also records that in the first year of {{ill|lt=Ninju|Q1373077|WD=Q1373077}} ({{ill|lt=851|Q29966|WD=Q29966}}), the shrine was granted the divine rank of Senior Sixth Rank Upper{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. In the *{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}}* {{ill|lt=Register of Deities|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}} compiled in the 5th year of {{ill|lt=Enchō|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Enchō}} ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470}}), it is listed as “Oiso Shrine” in {{ill|lt=Gamo District|Q931658|WD=Q931658}}, {{ill|lt=Ōmi Province|Q843882|WD=Q843882}}, and is ranked as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}}{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. In the 9th year of {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} ({{ill|lt=1581|Q6717|WD=Q6717}}), the main hall was rebuilt. In the {{ill|lt=early modern period|Q5308718|WD=Q5308718}}, it was known by names such as “Kama Daimyōjin” and “Kama-no-miya Shrine”{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. After the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707}}, under the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534}}, it was designated a village shrine in October {{ill|lt=1876|Q7766|WD=Q7766}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 9){{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. It was promoted to a township shrine in February {{ill|lt=1881|Q7808|WD=Q7808}} (Meiji 14), designated a shrine receiving state offerings in April {{ill|lt=1908|Q2056|WD=Q2056}} (Meiji 41), and in February {{ill|lt=1924|Q2173|WD=Q2173}} ({{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174}} 13) promoted to a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} and renamed its current name, “Oiso Shrine” [http://www.shiga-jinjacho.jp/ycBBS/Board.cgi/02_jinja_db/db/ycDB_02jinja-pc-detail.html?mode:view=1&view:oid=653 Oiso Shrine] (Shiga Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines).. During the {{ill|lt=period of rapid economic growth|Q1135329|WD=Q1135329}}, parts of the precincts were taken for the {{ill|lt=National Route 8|Q640239|WD=Q640239}} bypass and the {{ill|lt=Tōkaidō Shinkansen|Q660895|WD=Q660895}}, resulting in the precincts being divided. === Precincts === [[File:Oiso-jinja, honden.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Main hall (right, a National {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622}})
Suwa Shrine (left, a cultural property designated by Ōmihachiman City)}}]] [[File:Oiso-jinja, sandou.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Approach and shrine grove (Oisonomori)}}]] * Main hall ({{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622}}) – Rebuilt in the 9th year of {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} ({{ill|lt=1581|Q6717|WD=Q6717}}). It is a three-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} structure with a one-bay front porch and a cypress-bark roof. It features splendid decorations such as openwork arabesque carvings on the {{ill|lt=kamoi brackets|Q123117911|WD=Q123117911}}, carvings on the {{ill|lt=tesaki|Q124660898|WD=Q124660898}}, and latticework panels around the lower walls of the core. According to the {{ill|lt=ridge tag|Q8008834|WD=Q8008834}}, it was constructed by Shibata Iehisa, and a connection has been suggested with policies related to the formation of the castle town of {{ill|lt=Azuchi Castle|Q786888|WD=Q786888}} under {{ill|lt=Oda Nobunaga|Q171411|WD=Q171411}}Explanation board for the Oiso Shrine main hall within the precincts.. * Middle gate * Worship hall * Kagura hall * Shrine office === Oisonomori === A nationally designated {{ill|lt=Historic Site|Q1081138|WD=Q1081138}}. The shrine grove surrounding the buildings is called “Oisonomori.” The present forest consists mainly of {{ill|lt=cedar|Q147388|WD=Q147388}}, {{ill|lt=cypress|Q917187|WD=Q917187}}, and {{ill|lt=pine|Q12024|WD=Q12024}}. According to tradition in the *Oiso Shrine Honki*, the land was once split open with water welling up and was uninhabitable, but when Ishibe no Ōmuraji planted saplings and prayed to the gods, it became a great forest; Ishibe no Ōmuraji lived to over a hundred years, and thus it came to be called the “Oiso (Old and Revived) Forest.” The name appears in a poem from the first year of {{ill|lt=Manju|Q1194155|WD=Q1194155}} ({{ill|lt=1024|Q19265|WD=Q19265}}) in the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292}}, and it is also introduced as a {{ill|lt=utamakura|Q1588579|WD=Q1588579}} of Ōmi Province in works such as the *{{ill|lt=Ryōjin Hishō|Q3010584|WD=Q3010584}}*, *Nōin Utamakura*, and *Waka Shogakushō*. Because it lay along the medieval {{ill|lt=Tōkaidō|Q1196306|WD=Q1196306}} and the early modern Nakasendō, it is also mentioned in travelogues from the {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|WD=Q236205}} onward, such as the *{{ill|lt=Tōkan Kikō|Q11528660|WD=Q11528660}}*. For these reasons, as a place of long-standing and valuable history, the forest area has been designated a National Historic Site{{Sfn|Oisonomori (Heibonsha)|1991}}Explanation board for Oisonomori within the precincts.. File:Oiso-jinja, haiden.jpg|Worship hall File:Oiso-jinja, kaguraden.jpg|Kagura hall File:Oiso-jinja, enkei.jpg|Shrine grove (Oisonomori) seen from the former {{ill|lt=Nakasendō|Q1154479|WD=Q1154479}} === Subsidiary shrines === * Suwa Shrine (Tangible Cultural Property designated by Ōmihachiman City) *: Enshrined deities: {{ill|WD=Q9150391|lt=Takeminakata|Q9150391|WD=Q9150391|WD=Q9150391|lt=Takeminakata-no-Mikoto}}, {{ill|lt=Yasakatome|Q9150392|WD=Q9150392|lt=Yasakatome-no-Mikoto}}. Located to the west of the main hall. Although the exact construction date is unknown, it is thought to have been built around the Azuchi–Momoyama period. It is a one-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} structure with a cypress-bark roof. Repairs were carried out in the 7th year of {{ill|lt=Ansei|Q1327259|WD=Q1327259}} ({{ill|lt=1860|Q7691|WD=Q7691}}) and in {{ill|lt=1880|Q7806|WD=Q7806}} (Meiji 13), and it is regarded as a building that retains much of its original materials in good conditionExplanation board for the Suwa Shrine main hall within the Oiso Shrine precincts.. * Yoshizumi Inari Shrine File:Oiso-jinja, Yoshizumi-Inarisha.jpg|Yoshizumi Inari Shrine === Cultural properties === === Important Cultural Properties === * Main hall (structure) – Built in the Azuchi–Momoyama period, Tenshō 9 ({{ill|lt=1581|Q6717|WD=Q6717}}). Designated on April 17, {{ill|lt=1902|Q2043|WD=Q2043}} (Meiji 35){{National Cultural Properties Database|102|1474|Oiso Shrine Main Hall}}. === National Historic Sites === * Oisonomori – Designated on July 13, {{ill|lt=1949|Q5188|WD=Q5188}} ({{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567}} 24){{National Cultural Properties Database|401|1590|Oisonomori}}. === Cultural properties designated by Ōmihachiman City === * Tangible Cultural Properties ** Suwa Shrine main hall (structure) – Built around the Azuchi–Momoyama period. === Appearances in literature === * From the *{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Goshūi Wakashū|1=UNKNOWN}}*, a poem composed by Ōe no Kinsuke, Governor of Sagami, upon departing for his post in the first year of {{ill|lt=Manju|Q1194155|WD=Q1194155}} ({{ill|lt=1024|Q19265|WD=Q19265}}) {{Cquote|On the eastern road,
I shall keep it as a memory—
the cuckoo,
one cry in the deep night
of '''Oisonomori'''|20px||*{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Goshūi Wakashū|1=UNKNOWN}}* {{Sfn|Oisonomori (Heibonsha)|1991}}}} === On-site information === '''Address''' * Higashi-Oiso 1615, {{ill|lt=Azuchi Town|Q165676|WD=Q165676}}, {{ill|lt=Ōmihachiman City|Q385461|WD=Q385461}}, {{ill|lt=Shiga Prefecture|Q131358|WD=Q131358}} '''Access''' * About 15 minutes by {{ill|lt=Akakon Bus|Q17190918|WD=Q17190918}} from {{ill|lt=Azuchi Station|Q649689|WD=Q649689}} on the {{ill|lt=JR West|Q502125|WD=Q502125|lt=JR}} {{ill|lt=Tōkaidō Main Line|Q1190152|WD=Q1190152}} ({{ill|lt=Biwako Shrine precincts |Location={{ill|lt=Shiga Prefecture|Q131358|WD=Q131358}}{{ill|lt=Ōmihachiman City|Q385461|WD=Q385461}}{{ill|lt=Azuchi Town|Q165676|WD=Q165676}} Higashi-Oiso 1615 |Coordinates= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Deity={{ill|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|Q2564508|WD=Q2564508|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto}} |Shintai= |Shrine rank={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} (minor)
Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} |Founded=Traditionally: Year 5 of {{ill|lt=Emperor Kōrei|Q313041|WD=Q313041}} |Main hall=Three-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} |Alternate name=Kama-no-miya Shrine |Pilgrimage= |Annual festival=An auspicious day in April |Rituals= |Map= {{Location map|Japan Shiga|label=Oiso Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=250}} }} [[File:Oiso-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii gate}}]] '''Oiso Shrine''' (おいそじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in Higashi-Oiso, {{ill|lt=Azuchi Town|Q165676|WD=Q165676}}, {{ill|lt=Ōmihachiman City|Q385461|WD=Q385461}}, {{ill|lt=Shiga Prefecture|Q131358|WD=Q131358}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}}, and under the {{ill|lt=modern system of shrine rankings|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former ranking}} it was a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. It is enshrined within Oisonomori (老蘇森), a nationally designated {{ill|lt=Historic Site|Q1081138|WD=Q1081138}}, along the {{ill|lt=Nakasendō|Q1154479|WD=Q1154479}} (also called the Nakasendō), north of the Higashi-Oiso settlement. === Enshrined deity === The currently enshrined deity is the following single kamiExplanation board within the precincts.. * {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|Q2564508|WD=Q2564508|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto}} The various deities enshrined in the main hall are hearth-fire prevention deities. Because the character for “kama” (sickle) was applied to “kamado” (hearth), the shrine came to be called “Kama Daimyōjin” or “Kama-no-miya.” Even today, parishioners maintain the custom of offering sickles to the shrine{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. === History === === Foundation === According to the *Oiso Shrine Honki* (colophon dated September of the first year of {{ill|lt=Shitoku (Japan)|Q1146202|WD=Q1146202|lt=Shitoku}} ({{ill|lt=1384|Q6347|WD=Q6347}})), the shrine began in the 5th year of the 7th emperor, {{ill|lt=Emperor Kōrei|Q313041|WD=Q313041}}, when Ishibe no Ōmuraji built a sacred altar in Oisonomori and enshrined the child deity of Ōtoshi-no-Kami{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. It further states that during the reign of the 10th emperor, {{ill|lt=Emperor Sujin|Q333530|WD=Q333530}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kibitsuhiko|1=UNKNOWN}} established a palace and enshrined Okutsuhiko-no-Mikoto and Okutsuhime-no-Mikoto, and that during the reign of the 15th emperor, {{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997}}, Kasa no Ason (a descendant of Kibitsuhiko) additionally enshrined {{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru}} and {{ill|lt=Oto Tachibana-hime|Q11487396|WD=Q11487396|lt=Tachibana-hime}}{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. One theory holds that the shrine originated in primitive mountain worship with Mount Kinugasa as the {{ill|lt=shintai-zan|Q1595289|WD=Q1595289}} (sacred mountain), and that the present site is a village shrine from which the {{ill|lt=iwakura|Q11587880|WD=Q11587880}} at the mountain summit is worshiped from afar{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. === Overview === In the *{{ill|lt=Shinshō Kakuchōfushō|Q11502312|WD=Q11502312}}* record dated the first year of {{ill|lt=Daido (Japan)|Q1373191|WD=Q1373191|lt=Daidō}} ({{ill|lt=806|Q30021|WD=Q30021}}), one household from Ōmi Province was assigned as {{ill|lt=shinko (household)|Q11589711|WD=Q11589711}} to the then “Oiso Deity”{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The *Oiso Shrine Honki* also records that in the first year of {{ill|lt=Ninju|Q1373077|WD=Q1373077}} ({{ill|lt=851|Q29966|WD=Q29966}}), the shrine was granted the divine rank of Senior Sixth Rank Upper{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. In the *{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}}* {{ill|lt=Register of Deities|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}} compiled in the 5th year of {{ill|lt=Enchō|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Enchō}} ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470}}), it is listed as “Oiso Shrine” in {{ill|lt=Gamo District|Q931658|WD=Q931658}}, {{ill|lt=Ōmi Province|Q843882|WD=Q843882}}, and is ranked as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}}{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. In the 9th year of {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} ({{ill|lt=1581|Q6717|WD=Q6717}}), the main hall was rebuilt. In the {{ill|lt=early modern period|Q5308718|WD=Q5308718}}, it was known by names such as “Kama Daimyōjin” and “Kama-no-miya Shrine”{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. After the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707}}, under the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534}}, it was designated a village shrine in October {{ill|lt=1876|Q7766|WD=Q7766}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 9){{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. It was promoted to a township shrine in February {{ill|lt=1881|Q7808|WD=Q7808}} (Meiji 14), designated a shrine receiving state offerings in April {{ill|lt=1908|Q2056|WD=Q2056}} (Meiji 41), and in February {{ill|lt=1924|Q2173|WD=Q2173}} ({{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174}} 13) promoted to a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} and renamed its current name, “Oiso Shrine” [http://www.shiga-jinjacho.jp/ycBBS/Board.cgi/02_jinja_db/db/ycDB_02jinja-pc-detail.html?mode:view=1&view:oid=653 Oiso Shrine] (Shiga Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines).. During the {{ill|lt=period of rapid economic growth|Q1135329|WD=Q1135329}}, parts of the precincts were taken for the {{ill|lt=National Route 8|Q640239|WD=Q640239}} bypass and the {{ill|lt=Tōkaidō Shinkansen|Q660895|WD=Q660895}}, resulting in the precincts being divided. === Precincts === [[File:Oiso-jinja, honden.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Main hall (right, a National {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622}})
Suwa Shrine (left, a cultural property designated by Ōmihachiman City)}}]] [[File:Oiso-jinja, sandou.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Approach and shrine grove (Oisonomori)}}]] * Main hall ({{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622}}) – Rebuilt in the 9th year of {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} ({{ill|lt=1581|Q6717|WD=Q6717}}). It is a three-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} structure with a one-bay front porch and a cypress-bark roof. It features splendid decorations such as openwork arabesque carvings on the {{ill|lt=kamoi brackets|Q123117911|WD=Q123117911}}, carvings on the {{ill|lt=tesaki|Q124660898|WD=Q124660898}}, and latticework panels around the lower walls of the core. According to the {{ill|lt=ridge tag|Q8008834|WD=Q8008834}}, it was constructed by Shibata Iehisa, and a connection has been suggested with policies related to the formation of the castle town of {{ill|lt=Azuchi Castle|Q786888|WD=Q786888}} under {{ill|lt=Oda Nobunaga|Q171411|WD=Q171411}}Explanation board for the Oiso Shrine main hall within the precincts.. * Middle gate * Worship hall * Kagura hall * Shrine office === Oisonomori === A nationally designated {{ill|lt=Historic Site|Q1081138|WD=Q1081138}}. The shrine grove surrounding the buildings is called “Oisonomori.” The present forest consists mainly of {{ill|lt=cedar|Q147388|WD=Q147388}}, {{ill|lt=cypress|Q917187|WD=Q917187}}, and {{ill|lt=pine|Q12024|WD=Q12024}}. According to tradition in the *Oiso Shrine Honki*, the land was once split open with water welling up and was uninhabitable, but when Ishibe no Ōmuraji planted saplings and prayed to the gods, it became a great forest; Ishibe no Ōmuraji lived to over a hundred years, and thus it came to be called the “Oiso (Old and Revived) Forest.” The name appears in a poem from the first year of {{ill|lt=Manju|Q1194155|WD=Q1194155}} ({{ill|lt=1024|Q19265|WD=Q19265}}) in the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292}}, and it is also introduced as a {{ill|lt=utamakura|Q1588579|WD=Q1588579}} of Ōmi Province in works such as the *{{ill|lt=Ryōjin Hishō|Q3010584|WD=Q3010584}}*, *Nōin Utamakura*, and *Waka Shogakushō*. Because it lay along the medieval {{ill|lt=Tōkaidō|Q1196306|WD=Q1196306}} and the early modern Nakasendō, it is also mentioned in travelogues from the {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|WD=Q236205}} onward, such as the *{{ill|lt=Tōkan Kikō|Q11528660|WD=Q11528660}}*. For these reasons, as a place of long-standing and valuable history, the forest area has been designated a National Historic Site{{Sfn|Oisonomori (Heibonsha)|1991}}Explanation board for Oisonomori within the precincts.. File:Oiso-jinja, haiden.jpg|Worship hall File:Oiso-jinja, kaguraden.jpg|Kagura hall File:Oiso-jinja, enkei.jpg|Shrine grove (Oisonomori) seen from the former {{ill|lt=Nakasendō|Q1154479|WD=Q1154479}} === Subsidiary shrines === * Suwa Shrine (Tangible Cultural Property designated by Ōmihachiman City) *: Enshrined deities: {{ill|WD=Q9150391|lt=Takeminakata|Q9150391|WD=Q9150391|WD=Q9150391|lt=Takeminakata-no-Mikoto}}, {{ill|lt=Yasakatome|Q9150392|WD=Q9150392|lt=Yasakatome-no-Mikoto}}. Located to the west of the main hall. Although the exact construction date is unknown, it is thought to have been built around the Azuchi–Momoyama period. It is a one-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} structure with a cypress-bark roof. Repairs were carried out in the 7th year of {{ill|lt=Ansei|Q1327259|WD=Q1327259}} ({{ill|lt=1860|Q7691|WD=Q7691}}) and in {{ill|lt=1880|Q7806|WD=Q7806}} (Meiji 13), and it is regarded as a building that retains much of its original materials in good conditionExplanation board for the Suwa Shrine main hall within the Oiso Shrine precincts.. * Yoshizumi Inari Shrine File:Oiso-jinja, Yoshizumi-Inarisha.jpg|Yoshizumi Inari Shrine === Cultural properties === === Important Cultural Properties === * Main hall (structure) – Built in the Azuchi–Momoyama period, Tenshō 9 ({{ill|lt=1581|Q6717|WD=Q6717}}). Designated on April 17, {{ill|lt=1902|Q2043|WD=Q2043}} (Meiji 35){{National Cultural Properties Database|102|1474|Oiso Shrine Main Hall}}. === National Historic Sites === * Oisonomori – Designated on July 13, {{ill|lt=1949|Q5188|WD=Q5188}} ({{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567}} 24){{National Cultural Properties Database|401|1590|Oisonomori}}. === Cultural properties designated by Ōmihachiman City === * Tangible Cultural Properties ** Suwa Shrine main hall (structure) – Built around the Azuchi–Momoyama period. === Appearances in literature === * From the *{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Goshūi Wakashū|1=UNKNOWN}}*, a poem composed by Ōe no Kinsuke, Governor of Sagami, upon departing for his post in the first year of {{ill|lt=Manju|Q1194155|WD=Q1194155}} ({{ill|lt=1024|Q19265|WD=Q19265}}) {{Cquote|On the eastern road,
I shall keep it as a memory—
the cuckoo,
one cry in the deep night
of '''Oisonomori'''|20px||*{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Goshūi Wakashū|1=UNKNOWN}}* {{Sfn|Oisonomori (Heibonsha)|1991}}}} === On-site information === '''Address''' * Higashi-Oiso 1615, {{ill|lt=Azuchi Town|Q165676|WD=Q165676}}, {{ill|lt=Ōmihachiman City|Q385461|WD=Q385461}}, {{ill|lt=Shiga Prefecture|Q131358|WD=Q131358}} '''Access''' * About 15 minutes by {{ill|lt=Akakon Bus|Q17190918|WD=Q17190918}} from {{ill|lt=Azuchi Station|Q649689|WD=Q649689}} on the {{ill|lt=JR West|Q502125|WD=Q502125|lt=JR}} {{ill|lt=Tōkaidō Main Line|Q1190152|WD=Q1190152}} ({{ill|lt=Biwako Line|Q804135|WD=Q804135}}); get off at the Oiso Shrine stop. === Notes === {{Help notes}} {{Reflist|2}} === References === * On-site explanation boards * {{Cite book|Japanese|editor=Edited by the Meiji Shrine Records Compilation Office|author=|year=1912|chapter=Kama-no-miya Shrine|title={{ill|lt=Fuken Gōsha Meiji Jinja Shiryō|Q11486103|WD=Q11486103}}|publisher=Meiji Shrine Records Compilation Office|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Meiji Shrine Records|1912}}}} ** [{{NDLDC|1088278/101}} *Meiji Shrine Records: Prefectural and Township Shrines, Vol. 1*] (National Diet Library Digital Collection), reference frame 101. * {{Cite book|Japanese|editor=|author=|year=1991|chapter=|title={{ill|lt=Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|Q17213509|WD=Q17213509}} 25 Place Names of Shiga Prefecture|publisher={{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|WD=Q11482108}}|isbn=4582490255|ref=}} ** {{Wikicite|reference="Oiso Shrine"|ref={{Harvid|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}}}, {{Wikicite|reference="Oisonomori"|ref={{Harvid|Oisonomori (Heibonsha)|1991}}}}. * {{PDFlink|[http://www.shiga-ec.ed.jp/www/contents/1438304524592/html/common/other/55d173d3029.pdf “Oisonomori”]}} Shiga Prefectural Board of Education, Cultural Properties Protection Division, 2004 (Shiga Prefectural Comprehensive Education Center “Shiga Prefecture Cultural Properties Learning Sheet”). === External links === {{Commonscat|Oiso-jinja}} * [http://www.shiga-jinjacho.jp/ycBBS/Board.cgi/02_jinja_db/db/ycDB_02jinja-pc-detail.html?mode:view=1&view:oid=653 Oiso Shrine] – Shiga Prefecture Shrine precincts |Location={{ill|lt=Shiga Prefecture|Q131358|WD=Q131358|1=Shiga Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Ōmihachiman City|Q385461|WD=Q385461|1=Ōmihachiman}}{{ill|lt=Azuchi Town|Q165676|WD=Q165676|1=Azuchi, Shiga}} Higashi-Oiso 1615 |Coordinates= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Deity={{ill|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|Q2564508|WD=Q2564508|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|1=Ame-no-Koyane}} |Shintai= |Shrine rank={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (minor)
Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} |Founded=Traditionally: Year 5 of {{ill|lt=Emperor Kōrei|Q313041|WD=Q313041|1=Emperor Kōrei}} |Main hall=Three-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |Alternate name=Kama-no-miya Shrine |Pilgrimage= |Annual festival=An auspicious day in April |Rituals= |Map= {{Location map|Japan Shiga|label=Oiso Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=250}} }} [[File:Oiso-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii gate}}]] '''Oiso Shrine''' (おいそじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in Higashi-Oiso, {{ill|lt=Azuchi Town|Q165676|WD=Q165676|1=Azuchi, Shiga}}, {{ill|lt=Ōmihachiman City|Q385461|WD=Q385461|1=Ōmihachiman}}, {{ill|lt=Shiga Prefecture|Q131358|WD=Q131358|1=Shiga Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, and under the {{ill|lt=modern system of shrine rankings|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former ranking|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} it was a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. It is enshrined within Oisonomori (老蘇森), a nationally designated {{ill|lt=Historic Site|Q1081138|WD=Q1081138|1=Historic site}}, along the {{ill|lt=Nakasendō|Q1154479|WD=Q1154479|1=Nakasendō}} (also called the Nakasendō), north of the Higashi-Oiso settlement. === Enshrined deity === The currently enshrined deity is the following single kamiExplanation board within the precincts.. * {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|Q2564508|WD=Q2564508|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|1=Ame-no-Koyane}} The various deities enshrined in the main hall are hearth-fire prevention deities. Because the character for “kama” (sickle) was applied to “kamado” (hearth), the shrine came to be called “Kama Daimyōjin” or “Kama-no-miya.” Even today, parishioners maintain the custom of offering sickles to the shrine{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. === History === === Foundation === According to the *Oiso Shrine Honki* (colophon dated September of the first year of {{ill|lt=Shitoku (Japan)|Q1146202|WD=Q1146202|lt=Shitoku|1=Shitoku}} ({{ill|lt=1384|Q6347|WD=Q6347|1=1384}})), the shrine began in the 5th year of the 7th emperor, {{ill|lt=Emperor Kōrei|Q313041|WD=Q313041|1=Emperor Kōrei}}, when Ishibe no Ōmuraji built a sacred altar in Oisonomori and enshrined the child deity of Ōtoshi-no-Kami{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. It further states that during the reign of the 10th emperor, {{ill|lt=Emperor Sujin|Q333530|WD=Q333530|1=Emperor Sujin}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kibitsuhiko|1=UNKNOWN}} established a palace and enshrined Okutsuhiko-no-Mikoto and Okutsuhime-no-Mikoto, and that during the reign of the 15th emperor, {{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|1=Emperor Ōjin}}, Kasa no Ason (a descendant of Kibitsuhiko) additionally enshrined {{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}} and {{ill|lt=Oto Tachibana-hime|Q11487396|WD=Q11487396|lt=Tachibana-hime|1=Tachibanahime-no-Mikoto}}{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. One theory holds that the shrine originated in primitive mountain worship with Mount Kinugasa as the {{ill|lt=shintai-zan|Q1595289|WD=Q1595289|1=Sacred mountains}} (sacred mountain), and that the present site is a village shrine from which the {{ill|lt=iwakura|Q11587880|WD=Q11587880|1=Iwakura rock}} at the mountain summit is worshiped from afar{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. === Overview === In the *{{ill|lt=Shinshō Kakuchōfushō|Q11502312|WD=Q11502312|1=Shinshō Kyakuchokufushō}}* record dated the first year of {{ill|lt=Daido (Japan)|Q1373191|WD=Q1373191|lt=Daidō|1=Daidō}} ({{ill|lt=806|Q30021|WD=Q30021|1=806}}), one household from Ōmi Province was assigned as {{ill|lt=shinko (household)|Q11589711|WD=Q11589711|1=Kanbe (social class)}} to the then “Oiso Deity”{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The *Oiso Shrine Honki* also records that in the first year of {{ill|lt=Ninju|Q1373077|WD=Q1373077|1=Ninju}} ({{ill|lt=851|Q29966|WD=Q29966|1=851}}), the shrine was granted the divine rank of Senior Sixth Rank Upper{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. In the *{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}* {{ill|lt=Register of Deities|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} compiled in the 5th year of {{ill|lt=Enchō|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Enchō|1=Enchō}} ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470|1=927}}), it is listed as “Oiso Shrine” in {{ill|lt=Gamo District|Q931658|WD=Q931658|1=Gamō District, Shiga}}, {{ill|lt=Ōmi Province|Q843882|WD=Q843882|1=Ōmi Province}}, and is ranked as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. In the 9th year of {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} ({{ill|lt=1581|Q6717|WD=Q6717|1=1581}}), the main hall was rebuilt. In the {{ill|lt=early modern period|Q5308718|WD=Q5308718|1=Early modern period}}, it was known by names such as “Kama Daimyōjin” and “Kama-no-miya Shrine”{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. After the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707|1=Meiji Restoration}}, under the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, it was designated a village shrine in October {{ill|lt=1876|Q7766|WD=Q7766|1=1876}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 9){{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. It was promoted to a township shrine in February {{ill|lt=1881|Q7808|WD=Q7808|1=1881}} (Meiji 14), designated a shrine receiving state offerings in April {{ill|lt=1908|Q2056|WD=Q2056|1=1908}} (Meiji 41), and in February {{ill|lt=1924|Q2173|WD=Q2173|1=1924}} ({{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}} 13) promoted to a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} and renamed its current name, “Oiso Shrine” [http://www.shiga-jinjacho.jp/ycBBS/Board.cgi/02_jinja_db/db/ycDB_02jinja-pc-detail.html?mode:view=1&view:oid=653 Oiso Shrine] (Shiga Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines).. During the {{ill|lt=period of rapid economic growth|Q1135329|WD=Q1135329|1=1983 South American Rugby Championship}}, parts of the precincts were taken for the {{ill|lt=National Route 8|Q640239|WD=Q640239|1=Japan National Route 8}} bypass and the {{ill|lt=Tōkaidō Shinkansen|Q660895|WD=Q660895|1=Tokaido Shinkansen}}, resulting in the precincts being divided. === Precincts === [[File:Oiso-jinja, honden.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Main hall (right, a National {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}})
Suwa Shrine (left, a cultural property designated by Ōmihachiman City)}}]] [[File:Oiso-jinja, sandou.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Approach and shrine grove (Oisonomori)}}]] * Main hall ({{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}) – Rebuilt in the 9th year of {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} ({{ill|lt=1581|Q6717|WD=Q6717|1=1581}}). It is a three-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} structure with a one-bay front porch and a cypress-bark roof. It features splendid decorations such as openwork arabesque carvings on the {{ill|lt=kamoi brackets|Q123117911|WD=Q123117911|1=kaerumata}}, carvings on the {{ill|lt=tesaki|Q124660898|WD=Q124660898|1=tabasami}}, and latticework panels around the lower walls of the core. According to the {{ill|lt=ridge tag|Q8008834|WD=Q8008834|1=Munafuda}}, it was constructed by Shibata Iehisa, and a connection has been suggested with policies related to the formation of the castle town of {{ill|lt=Azuchi Castle|Q786888|WD=Q786888|1=Azuchi Castle}} under {{ill|lt=Oda Nobunaga|Q171411|WD=Q171411|1=Oda Nobunaga}}Explanation board for the Oiso Shrine main hall within the precincts.. * Middle gate * Worship hall * Kagura hall * Shrine office === Oisonomori === A nationally designated {{ill|lt=Historic Site|Q1081138|WD=Q1081138|1=Historic site}}. The shrine grove surrounding the buildings is called “Oisonomori.” The present forest consists mainly of {{ill|lt=cedar|Q147388|WD=Q147388|1=Cryptomeria}}, {{ill|lt=cypress|Q917187|WD=Q917187|1=Chamaecyparis obtusa}}, and {{ill|lt=pine|Q12024|WD=Q12024|1=Pine}}. According to tradition in the *Oiso Shrine Honki*, the land was once split open with water welling up and was uninhabitable, but when Ishibe no Ōmuraji planted saplings and prayed to the gods, it became a great forest; Ishibe no Ōmuraji lived to over a hundred years, and thus it came to be called the “Oiso (Old and Revived) Forest.” The name appears in a poem from the first year of {{ill|lt=Manju|Q1194155|WD=Q1194155|1=Manju (era)}} ({{ill|lt=1024|Q19265|WD=Q19265|1=1024}}) in the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292|1=Heian period}}, and it is also introduced as a {{ill|lt=utamakura|Q1588579|WD=Q1588579|1=Utamakura}} of Ōmi Province in works such as the *{{ill|lt=Ryōjin Hishō|Q3010584|WD=Q3010584|1=Ryōjin Hishō}}*, *Nōin Utamakura*, and *Waka Shogakushō*. Because it lay along the medieval {{ill|lt=Tōkaidō|Q1196306|WD=Q1196306|1=Tōkaidō (region)}} and the early modern Nakasendō, it is also mentioned in travelogues from the {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|WD=Q236205|1=Kamakura period}} onward, such as the *{{ill|lt=Tōkan Kikō|Q11528660|WD=Q11528660|1=Tōkan Kikō}}*. For these reasons, as a place of long-standing and valuable history, the forest area has been designated a National Historic Site{{Sfn|Oisonomori (Heibonsha)|1991}}Explanation board for Oisonomori within the precincts.. File:Oiso-jinja, haiden.jpg|Worship hall File:Oiso-jinja, kaguraden.jpg|Kagura hall File:Oiso-jinja, enkei.jpg|Shrine grove (Oisonomori) seen from the former {{ill|lt=Nakasendō|Q1154479|WD=Q1154479|1=Nakasendō}} === Subsidiary shrines === * Suwa Shrine (Tangible Cultural Property designated by Ōmihachiman City) *: Enshrined deities: {{ill|WD=Q9150391|lt=Takeminakata|Q9150391|WD=Q9150391|WD=Q9150391|lt=Takeminakata-no-Mikoto|1=Takeminakata}}, {{ill|lt=Yasakatome|Q9150392|WD=Q9150392|lt=Yasakatome-no-Mikoto|1=Yasakatome}}. Located to the west of the main hall. Although the exact construction date is unknown, it is thought to have been built around the Azuchi–Momoyama period. It is a one-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} structure with a cypress-bark roof. Repairs were carried out in the 7th year of {{ill|lt=Ansei|Q1327259|WD=Q1327259|1=Ansei}} ({{ill|lt=1860|Q7691|WD=Q7691|1=1860}}) and in {{ill|lt=1880|Q7806|WD=Q7806|1=1880}} (Meiji 13), and it is regarded as a building that retains much of its original materials in good conditionExplanation board for the Suwa Shrine main hall within the Oiso Shrine precincts.. * Yoshizumi Inari Shrine File:Oiso-jinja, Yoshizumi-Inarisha.jpg|Yoshizumi Inari Shrine === Cultural properties === === Important Cultural Properties === * Main hall (structure) – Built in the Azuchi–Momoyama period, Tenshō 9 ({{ill|lt=1581|Q6717|WD=Q6717|1=1581}}). Designated on April 17, {{ill|lt=1902|Q2043|WD=Q2043|1=1902}} (Meiji 35){{National Cultural Properties Database|102|1474|Oiso Shrine Main Hall}}. === National Historic Sites === * Oisonomori – Designated on July 13, {{ill|lt=1949|Q5188|WD=Q5188|1=1949}} ({{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 24){{National Cultural Properties Database|401|1590|Oisonomori}}. === Cultural properties designated by Ōmihachiman City === * Tangible Cultural Properties ** Suwa Shrine main hall (structure) – Built around the Azuchi–Momoyama period. === Appearances in literature === * From the *{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Goshūi Wakashū|1=UNKNOWN}}*, a poem composed by Ōe no Kinsuke, Governor of Sagami, upon departing for his post in the first year of {{ill|lt=Manju|Q1194155|WD=Q1194155|1=Manju (era)}} ({{ill|lt=1024|Q19265|WD=Q19265|1=1024}}) {{Cquote|On the eastern road,
I shall keep it as a memory—
the cuckoo,
one cry in the deep night
of '''Oisonomori'''|20px||*{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Goshūi Wakashū|1=UNKNOWN}}* {{Sfn|Oisonomori (Heibonsha)|1991}}}} === On-site information === '''Address''' * Higashi-Oiso 1615, {{ill|lt=Azuchi Town|Q165676|WD=Q165676|1=Azuchi, Shiga}}, {{ill|lt=Ōmihachiman City|Q385461|WD=Q385461|1=Ōmihachiman}}, {{ill|lt=Shiga Prefecture|Q131358|WD=Q131358|1=Shiga Prefecture}} '''Access''' * About 15 minutes by {{ill|lt=Akakon Bus|Q17190918|WD=Q17190918|1=Akakon Bus}} from {{ill|lt=Azuchi Station|Q649689|WD=Q649689|1=Azuchi Station}} on the {{ill|lt=JR West|Q502125|WD=Q502125|lt=JR|1=West Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|lt=Tōkaidō Main Line|Q1190152|WD=Q1190152|1=Tōkaidō Main Line}} ({{ill|lt=Biwako Line|Q804135|WD=Q804135|1=Biwako Line}}); get off at the Oiso Shrine stop. === Notes === {{Help Shrine precincts |Location={{ill|lt=Shiga Prefecture|Q131358|WD=Q131358|1=Shiga Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Ōmihachiman City|Q385461|WD=Q385461|1=Ōmihachiman}}{{ill|lt=Azuchi Town|Q165676|WD=Q165676|1=Azuchi, Shiga}} Higashi-Oiso 1615 |Coordinates= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Deity={{ill|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|Q2564508|WD=Q2564508|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|1=Ame-no-Koyane}} |Shintai= |Shrine rank={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (minor)
Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} |Founded=Traditionally: Year 5 of {{ill|lt=Emperor Kōrei|Q313041|WD=Q313041|1=Emperor Kōrei}} |Main hall=Three-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |Alternate name=Kama-no-miya Shrine |Pilgrimage= |Annual festival=An auspicious day in April |Rituals= |Map= {{Location map|Japan Shiga|label=Oiso Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=250}} }} [[File:Oiso-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii gate}}]] '''Oiso Shrine''' (おいそじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in Higashi-Oiso, {{ill|lt=Azuchi Town|Q165676|WD=Q165676|1=Azuchi, Shiga}}, {{ill|lt=Ōmihachiman City|Q385461|WD=Q385461|1=Ōmihachiman}}, {{ill|lt=Shiga Prefecture|Q131358|WD=Q131358|1=Shiga Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, and under the {{ill|lt=modern system of shrine rankings|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former ranking|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} it was a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. It is enshrined within Oisonomori (老蘇森), a nationally designated {{ill|lt=Historic Site|Q1081138|WD=Q1081138|1=Historic site}}, along the {{ill|lt=Nakasendō|Q1154479|WD=Q1154479|1=Nakasendō}} (also called the Nakasendō), north of the Higashi-Oiso settlement. === Enshrined deity === The currently enshrined deity is the following single kamiExplanation board within the precincts.. * {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|Q2564508|WD=Q2564508|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|1=Ame-no-Koyane}} The various deities enshrined in the main hall are hearth-fire prevention deities. Because the character for “kama” (sickle) was applied to “kamado” (hearth), the shrine came to be called “Kama Daimyōjin” or “Kama-no-miya.” Even today, parishioners maintain the custom of offering sickles to the shrine{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. === History === === Foundation === According to the *Oiso Shrine Honki* (colophon dated September of the first year of {{ill|lt=Shitoku (Japan)|Q1146202|WD=Q1146202|lt=Shitoku|1=Shitoku}} ({{ill|lt=1384|Q6347|WD=Q6347|1=1384}})), the shrine began in the 5th year of the 7th emperor, {{ill|lt=Emperor Kōrei|Q313041|WD=Q313041|1=Emperor Kōrei}}, when Ishibe no Ōmuraji built a sacred altar in Oisonomori and enshrined the child deity of Ōtoshi-no-Kami{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. It further states that during the reign of the 10th emperor, {{ill|lt=Emperor Sujin|Q333530|WD=Q333530|1=Emperor Sujin}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kibitsuhiko|1=UNKNOWN}} established a palace and enshrined Okutsuhiko-no-Mikoto and Okutsuhime-no-Mikoto, and that during the reign of the 15th emperor, {{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|1=Emperor Ōjin}}, Kasa no Ason (a descendant of Kibitsuhiko) additionally enshrined {{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}} and {{ill|lt=Oto Tachibana-hime|Q11487396|WD=Q11487396|lt=Tachibana-hime|1=Tachibanahime-no-Mikoto}}{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. One theory holds that the shrine originated in primitive mountain worship with Mount Kinugasa as the {{ill|lt=shintai-zan|Q1595289|WD=Q1595289|1=Sacred mountains}} (sacred mountain), and that the present site is a village shrine from which the {{ill|lt=iwakura|Q11587880|WD=Q11587880|1=Iwakura rock}} at the mountain summit is worshiped from afar{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. === Overview === In the *{{ill|lt=Shinshō Kakuchōfushō|Q11502312|WD=Q11502312|1=Shinshō Kyakuchokufushō}}* record dated the first year of {{ill|lt=Daido (Japan)|Q1373191|WD=Q1373191|lt=Daidō|1=Daidō}} ({{ill|lt=806|Q30021|WD=Q30021|1=806}}), one household from Ōmi Province was assigned as {{ill|lt=shinko (household)|Q11589711|WD=Q11589711|1=Kanbe (social class)}} to the then “Oiso Deity”{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The *Oiso Shrine Honki* also records that in the first year of {{ill|lt=Ninju|Q1373077|WD=Q1373077|1=Ninju}} ({{ill|lt=851|Q29966|WD=Q29966|1=851}}), the shrine was granted the divine rank of Senior Sixth Rank Upper{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. In the *{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}* {{ill|lt=Register of Deities|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} compiled in the 5th year of {{ill|lt=Enchō|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Enchō|1=Enchō}} ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470|1=927}}), it is listed as “Oiso Shrine” in {{ill|lt=Gamo District|Q931658|WD=Q931658|1=Gamō District, Shiga}}, {{ill|lt=Ōmi Province|Q843882|WD=Q843882|1=Ōmi Province}}, and is ranked as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. In the 9th year of {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} ({{ill|lt=1581|Q6717|WD=Q6717|1=1581}}), the main hall was rebuilt. In the {{ill|lt=early modern period|Q5308718|WD=Q5308718|1=Early modern period}}, it was known by names such as “Kama Daimyōjin” and “Kama-no-miya Shrine”{{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. After the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707|1=Meiji Restoration}}, under the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, it was designated a village shrine in October {{ill|lt=1876|Q7766|WD=Q7766|1=1876}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 9){{Sfn|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}. It was promoted to a township shrine in February {{ill|lt=1881|Q7808|WD=Q7808|1=1881}} (Meiji 14), designated a shrine receiving state offerings in April {{ill|lt=1908|Q2056|WD=Q2056|1=1908}} (Meiji 41), and in February {{ill|lt=1924|Q2173|WD=Q2173|1=1924}} ({{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}} 13) promoted to a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} and renamed its current name, “Oiso Shrine” [http://www.shiga-jinjacho.jp/ycBBS/Board.cgi/02_jinja_db/db/ycDB_02jinja-pc-detail.html?mode:view=1&view:oid=653 Oiso Shrine] (Shiga Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines).. During the {{ill|lt=period of rapid economic growth|Q1135329|WD=Q1135329|1=1983 South American Rugby Championship}}, parts of the precincts were taken for the {{ill|lt=National Route 8|Q640239|WD=Q640239|1=Japan National Route 8}} bypass and the {{ill|lt=Tōkaidō Shinkansen|Q660895|WD=Q660895|1=Tokaido Shinkansen}}, resulting in the precincts being divided. === Precincts === [[File:Oiso-jinja, honden.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Main hall (right, a National {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}})
Suwa Shrine (left, a cultural property designated by Ōmihachiman City)}}]] [[File:Oiso-jinja, sandou.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Approach and shrine grove (Oisonomori)}}]] * Main hall ({{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}) – Rebuilt in the 9th year of {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} ({{ill|lt=1581|Q6717|WD=Q6717|1=1581}}). It is a three-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} structure with a one-bay front porch and a cypress-bark roof. It features splendid decorations such as openwork arabesque carvings on the {{ill|lt=kamoi brackets|Q123117911|WD=Q123117911|1=kaerumata}}, carvings on the {{ill|lt=tesaki|Q124660898|WD=Q124660898|1=tabasami}}, and latticework panels around the lower walls of the core. According to the {{ill|lt=ridge tag|Q8008834|WD=Q8008834|1=Munafuda}}, it was constructed by Shibata Iehisa, and a connection has been suggested with policies related to the formation of the castle town of {{ill|lt=Azuchi Castle|Q786888|WD=Q786888|1=Azuchi Castle}} under {{ill|lt=Oda Nobunaga|Q171411|WD=Q171411|1=Oda Nobunaga}}Explanation board for the Oiso Shrine main hall within the precincts.. * Middle gate * Worship hall * Kagura hall * Shrine office === Oisonomori === A nationally designated {{ill|lt=Historic Site|Q1081138|WD=Q1081138|1=Historic site}}. The shrine grove surrounding the buildings is called “Oisonomori.” The present forest consists mainly of {{ill|lt=cedar|Q147388|WD=Q147388|1=Cryptomeria}}, {{ill|lt=cypress|Q917187|WD=Q917187|1=Chamaecyparis obtusa}}, and {{ill|lt=pine|Q12024|WD=Q12024|1=Pine}}. According to tradition in the *Oiso Shrine Honki*, the land was once split open with water welling up and was uninhabitable, but when Ishibe no Ōmuraji planted saplings and prayed to the gods, it became a great forest; Ishibe no Ōmuraji lived to over a hundred years, and thus it came to be called the “Oiso (Old and Revived) Forest.” The name appears in a poem from the first year of {{ill|lt=Manju|Q1194155|WD=Q1194155|1=Manju (era)}} ({{ill|lt=1024|Q19265|WD=Q19265|1=1024}}) in the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292|1=Heian period}}, and it is also introduced as a {{ill|lt=utamakura|Q1588579|WD=Q1588579|1=Utamakura}} of Ōmi Province in works such as the *{{ill|lt=Ryōjin Hishō|Q3010584|WD=Q3010584|1=Ryōjin Hishō}}*, *Nōin Utamakura*, and *Waka Shogakushō*. Because it lay along the medieval {{ill|lt=Tōkaidō|Q1196306|WD=Q1196306|1=Tōkaidō (region)}} and the early modern Nakasendō, it is also mentioned in travelogues from the {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|WD=Q236205|1=Kamakura period}} onward, such as the *{{ill|lt=Tōkan Kikō|Q11528660|WD=Q11528660|1=Tōkan Kikō}}*. For these reasons, as a place of long-standing and valuable history, the forest area has been designated a National Historic Site{{Sfn|Oisonomori (Heibonsha)|1991}}Explanation board for Oisonomori within the precincts.. File:Oiso-jinja, haiden.jpg|Worship hall File:Oiso-jinja, kaguraden.jpg|Kagura hall File:Oiso-jinja, enkei.jpg|Shrine grove (Oisonomori) seen from the former {{ill|lt=Nakasendō|Q1154479|WD=Q1154479|1=Nakasendō}} === Subsidiary shrines === * Suwa Shrine (Tangible Cultural Property designated by Ōmihachiman City) *: Enshrined deities: {{ill|WD=Q9150391|lt=Takeminakata|Q9150391|WD=Q9150391|WD=Q9150391|lt=Takeminakata-no-Mikoto|1=Takeminakata}}, {{ill|lt=Yasakatome|Q9150392|WD=Q9150392|lt=Yasakatome-no-Mikoto|1=Yasakatome}}. Located to the west of the main hall. Although the exact construction date is unknown, it is thought to have been built around the Azuchi–Momoyama period. It is a one-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} structure with a cypress-bark roof. Repairs were carried out in the 7th year of {{ill|lt=Ansei|Q1327259|WD=Q1327259|1=Ansei}} ({{ill|lt=1860|Q7691|WD=Q7691|1=1860}}) and in {{ill|lt=1880|Q7806|WD=Q7806|1=1880}} (Meiji 13), and it is regarded as a building that retains much of its original materials in good conditionExplanation board for the Suwa Shrine main hall within the Oiso Shrine precincts.. * Yoshizumi Inari Shrine File:Oiso-jinja, Yoshizumi-Inarisha.jpg|Yoshizumi Inari Shrine === Cultural properties === === Important Cultural Properties === * Main hall (structure) – Built in the Azuchi–Momoyama period, Tenshō 9 ({{ill|lt=1581|Q6717|WD=Q6717|1=1581}}). Designated on April 17, {{ill|lt=1902|Q2043|WD=Q2043|1=1902}} (Meiji 35){{National Cultural Properties Database|102|1474|Oiso Shrine Main Hall}}. === National Historic Sites === * Oisonomori – Designated on July 13, {{ill|lt=1949|Q5188|WD=Q5188|1=1949}} ({{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 24){{National Cultural Properties Database|401|1590|Oisonomori}}. === Cultural properties designated by Ōmihachiman City === * Tangible Cultural Properties ** Suwa Shrine main hall (structure) – Built around the Azuchi–Momoyama period. === Appearances in literature === * From the *{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Goshūi Wakashū|1=UNKNOWN}}*, a poem composed by Ōe no Kinsuke, Governor of Sagami, upon departing for his post in the first year of {{ill|lt=Manju|Q1194155|WD=Q1194155|1=Manju (era)}} ({{ill|lt=1024|Q19265|WD=Q19265|1=1024}}) {{Cquote|On the eastern road,
I shall keep it as a memory—
the cuckoo,
one cry in the deep night
of '''Oisonomori'''|20px||*{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Goshūi Wakashū|1=UNKNOWN}}* {{Sfn|Oisonomori (Heibonsha)|1991}}}} === On-site information === '''Address''' * Higashi-Oiso 1615, {{ill|lt=Azuchi Town|Q165676|WD=Q165676|1=Azuchi, Shiga}}, {{ill|lt=Ōmihachiman City|Q385461|WD=Q385461|1=Ōmihachiman}}, {{ill|lt=Shiga Prefecture|Q131358|WD=Q131358|1=Shiga Prefecture}} '''Access''' * About 15 minutes by {{ill|lt=Akakon Bus|Q17190918|WD=Q17190918|1=Akakon Bus}} from {{ill|lt=Azuchi Station|Q649689|WD=Q649689|1=Azuchi Station}} on the {{ill|lt=JR West|Q502125|WD=Q502125|lt=JR|1=West Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|lt=Tōkaidō Main Line|Q1190152|WD=Q1190152|1=Tōkaidō Main Line}} ({{ill|lt=Biwako Line|Q804135|WD=Q804135|1=Biwako Line}}); get off at the Oiso Shrine stop. === Notes === {{Help notes}} {{Reflist|2}} === References === * On-site explanation boards * {{Cite book|Japanese|editor=Edited by the Meiji Shrine Records Compilation Office|author=|year=1912|chapter=Kama-no-miya Shrine|title={{ill|lt=Fuken Gōsha Meiji Jinja Shiryō|Q11486103|WD=Q11486103|1=Fuken Gōsha Meiji Jinja Shiryō}}|publisher=Meiji Shrine Records Compilation Office|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Meiji Shrine Records|1912}}}} ** [{{NDLDC|1088278/101}} *Meiji Shrine Records: Prefectural and Township Shrines, Vol. 1*] (National Diet Library Digital Collection), reference frame 101. * {{Cite book|Japanese|editor=|author=|year=1991|chapter=|title={{ill|lt=Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|Q17213509|WD=Q17213509|1=Japanese Historical Topography Series}} 25 Place Names of Shiga Prefecture|publisher={{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|WD=Q11482108|1=Heibonsha}}|isbn=4582490255|ref=}} ** {{Wikicite|reference="Oiso Shrine"|ref={{Harvid|Oiso Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}}}, {{Wikicite|reference="Oisonomori"|ref={{Harvid|Oisonomori (Heibonsha)|1991}}}}. * {{PDFlink|[http://www.shiga-ec.ed.jp/www/contents/1438304524592/html/common/other/55d173d3029.pdf “Oisonomori”]}} Shiga Prefectural Board of Education, Cultural Properties Protection Division, 2004 (Shiga Prefectural Comprehensive Education Center “Shiga Prefecture Cultural Properties Learning Sheet”). ===
